text:
"Sun-Face Buddha, Moon-Face Buddha—our body, mind, and environs—are also within the moon. The coming and going of birth and death is also the moon. The entire world of the ten directions is the up and down, the left and right of the moon. Everyday activity at this moment is hundreds of grasses brilliant in the moon, the mind of ancestors brilliant in the moon."

- Dōgen, Shōbōgenzō 43, 'The Moon'

raw.full_text:

Everyday activity at this moment is hundreds of grasses brilliant in the moon, the mind of ancestors brilliant in the moon."

text:
"Without resistance in all four directions,
content with whatever you get,
enduring troubles without panic,
wander alone
like a rhinoceros.
...
We praise companionship
—yes!
Those on a par, or better,
should be chosen as friends.
If they’re not to be found,
living faultlessly,
wander alone
like a rhinoceros.
...
Transcending the contortion of views,
the sure way attained,
the path gained,
“Unled by others,
I have knowledge arisen,”
wander alone
like a rhinoceros.

With no greed, no deceit,
no thirst, no hypocrisy—
delusion & blemishes
blown away—
with no inclinations for all the world,
every world,
wander alone
like a rhinoceros.
...
Shattering fetters,
like a fish in the water tearing a net,
like a fire not coming back to what’s burnt,
wander alone
like a rhinoceros.
...
Having let go of passion,
aversion,
delusion;
having shattered the fetters;
unfazed at the ending of life,
wander alone
like a rhinoceros."

text:
Talking to a non social media user friend earlier today about how I use it deliberately got me reflecting on patterns of use. Here's one:
Sometimes I follow someone for an outlier good post and then unfollow afterwards because I'm not into the whole of the subsequent stream. Sometimes I dip in and out as people go through posting phases.
Unfollow ≠ dislike.
If I've unfollowed after following there's a good chance I'd still like to interact/make friends!
What's your pattern/strategy?

text:
Siddhartha Gautama's historically unique religious genius was not to mistake his dependently arisen altered states of consciousness for the ultimate nature of reality itself independent of his mind, to utterly see through to the very end the empty, selfless, fabricated nature of any and all perception, and to sever at the root all self-bound suffering therein.
The history of Buddhadharma since is largely an exploration of the state-space of possible deviations from his discomfiting conclusions.

text:
"Unstartled, like a lion at sounds.
Unsnared, like the wind in a net.
Unsmeared, like a lotus in water:
Wander alone
like a rhinoceros.

Like a lion—forceful, strong in fang,
living as a conqueror, the king of beasts—
resort to a solitary dwelling.
Wander alone
like a rhinoceros.

At the right time consorting
with the release through goodwill,
compassion,
empathetic joy,
equanimity,
unobstructed by all the world,
any world,
wander alone
like a rhinoceros."

text:
The Teaching on Striving According to Dhamma

"Bhikkus, the Dhamma has thus been well expounded by me, elucidated, disclosed, revealed, and stripped of patchwork — this is enough for a clansman who has gone forth out of faith, to arouse his energy thus:

'Willingly let only my skin, sinews, and bones remain, and let the flesh and blood in this body wither away. As long as whatever is to be attained by human strength, by human energy, by human effort, has not been attained, let not my efforts stand still.'

Bhikkus, the lazy person dwells in suffering, soiled by evil, unwholesome states, and great is the personal good that he neglects. The energetic person though dwells happily. Well withdrawn from unwholesome states, and great is the personal good that he achieves.

Bhikkus, it is not by lower means that the supreme is attained but, bhikkus, it is by the supreme that the supreme is attained. Bhikkus, this holy life is like the cream of the milk:

The Teacher is present, therefore, bhikkus, start to arouse your energy for the attainment of the as yet unattained, for the achievement of the as yet unachieved, for the realization of the as yet unrealized. Thinking, in such a way:

'Our Going Forth will not be barren but will become fruitful and fertile, and all our use of robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicinal requisites, given by others for our support, will reward them with great fruit and great benefit.'

Bhikkus, you should train yourselves thus:
Considering your own good, it is enough to strive for the goal without negligence; Bhikkus, considering the good of others, it is enough to strive for the goal without negligence; Bhikkus, considering the good of both, it is enough strive for the goal without negligence."

Bhikkus, it is not by lower means that the supreme is attained but, bhikkus, it is by the supreme that the supreme is attained. Bhikkus, this holy life is like the cream of the milk:

'Our Going Forth will not be barren but will become fruitful and fertile, and all our use of robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicinal requisites, given by others for our support, will reward them with great fruit and great benefit.'

Bhikkus, you should train yourselves thus:
Considering your own good, it is enough to strive for the goal without negligence; Bhikkus, considering the good of others, it is enough to strive for the goal without negligence; Bhikkus, considering the good of both, it is enough strive for the goal without negligence."

text:
A Matter of the Heart

Jagged edges run through this heart,
Here they're born and borne,
Here they dye and die.

This split is I,
Those cracks are karma,
That fault is mine.

Exhausting everything,
Fixing nothing,
In this unmade open evenness,
Only original perfection pervades.

Jagged edges run through every heart,

Starving, striving, writhing, wrath,
Slaughter at home,
Stirring, murmuring, rumbling, rising response,
Awake, awake!

Together:

Rising up,
Reaching down.
Pulling up,
Pushing forward.

text:
Ekstasis

Wind-chime bones,
Thin skin drapery,
Fickle flesh.

Unbound all round,
Sound sounds out,
Out!

Swallowing sky,
Spitting cloud,
Fluttering birds.

Two feet tap step,
Step,
Two hands up,
Up.

Rain spatters delight,
Obscuring eyeglasses,
Rainbow bright splashes.

Yes, yes, yes,
A thousand times no,
Yes!

text:
Contrition

Sky, boundless,
Awareness, groundless,
Soaring, trackless,

But really now,

So long as any trace remains,
Better to wipe myself with the sutras,
And smear myself with my own filth,
Than spout any more such nonsense.

text:
"Although beings’ true nature has always been awakened,
In their non-recognition, they wander in saṃsāra without end.
For these sentient beings whose misery knows no bounds,
May I develop a compassion that is unbearably intense.

The strength of unbearable compassion is unrestricted,
And in this love, the reality of the empty essence laid bare.
May I constantly cultivate this supreme, unerring path of unity
And may I never be apart from it, by day or by night."

- Rangjung Dorje, 3rd Karmapa

Pictured: Śrīdēvī/Palden Lhamo

text:
The traditional view of entities employed in Buddhist tantric practice is that they have as much extra-mental existence as you or me, which is to say that while their appearance in/as your own mind is just that, they are not solely that.
Epistemological idealism ≠ metaphysical idealism.
That said, you can engage them and their practice(s) without any determinate stance as to their ontology and AFAICT derive the full benefit. Likewise with views regarding rebirth and associated practices.
I don't know, myself.
Phenomenology and ethics first. Fine to forget the rest.

text:
Speaking of, come join me in person in NYC or online for an afternoon of deity yoga with Avalokiteśvara, here.

Saturday, August 30
In-Person: NYC, Online via Zoom
2PM - 6PM Eastern, 11AM - 3PM Pacific, 4PM - 8PM London

Receive unelaborate instruction to engage practice of the Great Compassionate One through mantra, imaginal and somatic enactment, and recognition of the nature of mind. Though a formal empowerment is not required for this practice, and I will not provide one, if you feel an affinity I encourage you afterwards to seek one out.

Intensive Format

- Opening hour for hellos, instruction, guided meditation, and Q&A.

- 2.5 hour practice period beginning with a longer guided meditation, during which I’ll be available for 1:1 sessions with participants.

- Concluding half-hour for reports, discussion, Q&A, goodbyes.

Link to register below.

text:
A shift: as far as my own presentation and teaching goes, not so interested in secular language anymore. I'm glad Shinzen and others have done and continue to advance that work, but it's not mine to contribute to this go round.

Though I'm not presently a representative of any of the particular Buddhist lineages I've engaged and continue to practice within, I've been encouraged by several of my more traditional teachers to go ahead outside the credential paradigm for now, and I intend to formally ordain in one or several of my teachers' lineages in coming years, anyhow.

So, I'm teaching what I understand and am qualified to transmit of Buddhadharma. I don't care if you identify as such, but that's what I have to offer to anyone interested.
🤲❤️‍🔥🙏

text:
“There is the case, Ānanda, where a disciple of the noble ones considers this: ‘Sensuality here & now; sensuality in lives to come; sensual perceptions here & now; sensual perceptions in lives to come; forms here & now; forms in lives to come; form-perceptions here & now; form-perceptions in lives to come; perceptions of the imperturbable; perceptions of the dimension of nothingness; perceptions of the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception: That is an identity, to the extent that there is an identity. This is deathless: the liberation of the mind through lack of clinging.’

...

I have taught the way to cross over the flood by going from one support to the next, the noble liberation. Whatever a teacher should do—seeking the welfare of his disciples, out of sympathy for them—that have I done for you. Over there are the roots of trees; over there, empty dwellings. Practice jhāna, Ānanda. Don’t be heedless. Don’t later fall into remorse. That is our message to you all.”

text: "Dharmakaya is grasping the so-called ultimate, the experience of utter and pure nondifferentiation and nonreification. Were someone to hand us something such as a clod of earth, from this standpoint there is no way to speak about it. There is no conceptualization or separation at all. This is the experience of the breakthrough koans, like Mu or “Who am I?” When the answer to the koan comes, it is a direct knowing of this [hits lectern]. Suddenly you know who and what you really are, what “it” is, wordlessly and without doubt. This is recognizing your nature; this is the awakening we call kensho. But we cannot get stuck in nondifferentiation, or we will never truly walk the bodhisattva path. Coming immediately out from it, we must experience the same clod of earth from the sambhogakaya viewpoint. All is boundless and empty of self, true, but this in our hands is precisely a clod of earth, luminously perfect as it is—only thus could the ultimate manifest. Using the language of some religions, this is the place where we could even say that a clod of earth is something holy, sacred, or magical, or that the holy and sacred manifests exactly as a clod of earth. This is our wisdom, and the training after kensho by which everything we encounter is revealed as not different from the original face of our true nature. Still, we cannot get stuck there either. We must experience from the nirmanakaya viewpoint. Here, the earth is just earth, and it is black, and it looks like it might be good for growing carrots. It holds up my feet when I walk. This is the wisdom realm of our everyday activities, our work, and our lives. With great compassion, we must enter fully into this world in which we will manifest the wondrous functioning of wisdom within daily activity and so apply myriad skillful means to help others. Coming full circle in our Zen training, we must in the end be completely ordinary, nothing special at all."

text:
I don't see stable practice developing and unbound freedom dawning in the absence of thorough disenchantment with samsara and concomitant urgency to trace the dis-ease and settle it at the sole source of this I, me, mine.

Missing that first exit, however much instruction you receive and practice you accumulate it likely won't penetrate and reach the root of the matter.

There are faster and slower routes home, but no shortcuts getting there, and scarce chance of making it if you're following directions and looking for signs of a road you're not even on.

text:
Not realizing it at the time, I had relied exclusively on fickle passions to provide motivation and direction selecting and sustaining intimate relationship. Then, when those died down somewhat and were inconsistently present at all I was dully confused as to why I couldn't bring myself to show up with constancy in the way I had promised I would.

There was never a good foundation based on an all-told assessment of compatibility, goals, mutual respect, and care.

No wonder it fell apart.

Finding myself falling in love now again, seeing more clearly, choosing more carefully, not rushing, not chasing, but waiting, watching, discerning amidst the burning what's real, what's good, what's true, and only then what to do.

text:
“I teach the Dhamma for the abandoning of the gross... the mind-made... the formless appropriation of a self, such that, when you practice it, defiling mental qualities will be abandoned, bright mental qualities will grow, and you will enter & remain in the culmination & abundance of discernment, having known & realized it for yourself in the here & now. If the thought should occur to you that, when defiling mental qualities are abandoned and bright mental qualities have grown, and one enters & remains in the culmination & abundance of discernment, having known & realized it for oneself in the here & now, one’s abiding is stressful/painful, you should not see it in that way. When defiling mental qualities are abandoned and bright mental qualities have grown, and one enters & remains in the culmination & abundance of discernment, having known & realized it for oneself in the here & now, there is joy, rapture, calm, mindfulness, alertness, and a pleasant/happy abiding.

...these are the world’s designations, the world’s expressions, the world’s ways of speaking, the world’s descriptions, with which the Tathāgata expresses himself but without grasping to them.”

raw.full_text:
“I teach the Dhamma for the abandoning of the gross... the mind-made... the formless appropriation of a self, such that, when you practice it, defiling mental qualities will be abandoned, bright mental qualities will grow, and you will enter & remain in the culmination & abundance of discernment, having known & realized it for yourself in the here & now. If the thought should occur to you that, when defiling mental qualities are abandoned and bright mental qualities have grown, and one enters & remains in the culmination & abundance of discernment, having known & realized it for oneself in the here & now, one’s abiding is stressful/painful, you should not see it in that way. When defiling mental qualities are abandoned and bright mental qualities have grown, and one enters & remains in the culmination & abundance of discernment, having known & realized it for oneself in the here & now, there is joy, rapture, calm, mindfulness, alertness, and a pleasant/happy abiding.

...these are the world’s designations, the world’s expressions, the world’s ways of speaking, the world’s descriptions, with which the Tathāgata expresses himself but without grasping to them.”

text:
You

You see straight through me.
I know.
I see so in your eyes.

You know men well.
I'm one of those, so,
Nothing much here will surprise.

Yet,

Your gaze catches,
On word, gesture, glance,
Delights, opens, invites,
Sighs, moans, laughing, love.

Awareness embraced,
Limbs entwined,
Falling in,
Fading out.

Stoked to blazing,
Burning what here remains.
Playing in ashes,
Dancing by emberlight.

text:
The crises we individually and collectively face are different in detail but not in kind from the fundamental predicament every human being and society has found itself in from the first:

Being born, death approaches, and in this interval of becoming activity is ordered by attraction and aversion animated by grasping to self, others, and objects in space and time. Within this regime of consciousness there is no release.

There is release, however, and from here perhaps we can cooperatively reshape our collective destiny for the benefit of all.

May it be,
May it be,
May it be.

text: "Consider for a moment how unlikely it is that practices developed by celibate recluses in South Asia over twenty-five centuries ago who renounced their possessions, homes, caste, and family identities to search their minds for a way to transcend sickness, ageing, and death—indeed the entire world—would be adapted to help middle-class professionals function better at work, bond with their families, manage their health, and find calm amid the frantic pace of modern life—even improve their golf game or sex life. How did this happen?"

raw.full_text: "Consider for a moment how unlikely it is that practices developed by celibate recluses in South Asia over twenty-five centuries ago who renounced their possessions, homes, caste, and family identities to search their minds for a way to transcend sickness, ageing, and death—indeed the entire world—would be adapted to help middle-class professionals function better at work, bond with their families, manage their health, and find calm amid the frantic pace of modern life—even improve their golf game or sex life. How did this happen?"

text: "What makes it through the filters of modernity are practices that can make intuitive sense and find a home within the categories in which we are accustomed to think, the affective domains in which we habitually feel, the aesthetic sensibilities through which we typically perceive things, and the social, political, and institutional realities within which we are embedded. These filters have allowed only a trickle of meditative practices through to the mainstream of our world."

raw.full_text: "What makes it through the filters of modernity are practices that can make intuitive sense and find a home within the categories in which we are accustomed to think, the affective domains in which we habitually feel, the aesthetic sensibilities through which we typically perceive things, and the social, political, and institutional realities within which we are embedded. These filters have allowed only a trickle of meditative practices through to the mainstream of our world."

text: "My purpose is not to complain that Buddhist meditation has become “contaminated” with such assumptions, or to insist that some version of “traditional” Buddhism is better, but rather to show that these assumptions are there and render them more distinct so that we can then evaluate them more precisely."

text:
Right Speech:

"[1] In the case of words that the Tathāgata knows to be unfactual, untrue, unbeneficial, unendearing & disagreeable to others, he does not say them.

[2] In the case of words that the Tathāgata knows to be factual, true, unbeneficial, unendearing & disagreeable to others, he does not say them.

[3] In the case of words that the Tathāgata knows to be factual, true, beneficial, but unendearing & disagreeable to others, he has a sense of the proper time for saying them.

[4] In the case of words that the Tathāgata knows to be unfactual, untrue, unbeneficial, but endearing & agreeable to others, he does not say them.

[5] In the case of words that the Tathāgata knows to be factual, true, unbeneficial, but endearing & agreeable to others, he does not say them.

[6] In the case of words that the Tathāgata knows to be factual, true, beneficial, and endearing & agreeable to others, he has a sense of the proper time for saying them. Why is that? Because the Tathāgata has sympathy for living beings.”

How to admonish another:

"O bhikkhus, a bhikkhu who desires to admonish another should do so after investigating five conditions in himself and after establishing five other conditions in himself. What are the five conditions which he should investigate in himself?

[1] "Am I one who practices purity in bodily action, flawless and untainted...?

[2] "Am I one who practices purity in speech, flawless and untainted...?

[3] "Is the heart of goodwill, free from malice, established in me towards fellow-farers in the holy life...?

[4] "Am I or am I not one who has heard much, who bears in mind what he has heard, who stores up what he has heard? Those teachings which are good alike in their beginning, middle, and ending, proclaiming perfectly the spirit and the letter of the utterly purified holy life — have such teachings been much heard by me, borne in mind, practiced in speech, pondered in the heart and rightly penetrated by insight...?

[5] "Are the Patimokkhas [rules of conduct for monks and nuns] in full thoroughly learned by heart, well-analyzed with thorough knowledge of their meanings, clearly divided sutta by sutta and known in minute detail by me...?

"These five conditions must be investigated in himself.

"And what other five conditions must be established in himself?

[1] "Do I speak at the right time, or not?

[2] "Do I speak of facts, or not?

[3] "Do I speak gently or harshly?

[4] "Do I speak profitable words or not?

[5] "Do I speak with a kindly heart, or inwardly malicious?

"O bhikkhus, these five conditions are to be investigated in himself and the latter five established in himself by a bhikkhu who desires to admonish another."

text:
When I was a young child living in a rural area on a small island, meeting new people was very exciting. When we would get on one of the larger ferries between islands I would introduce myself and try to get to know as many people as possible. I eventually became too self-conscious to keep up the habit.

Having dropped off much of that habit of self-consciousness, I appear to once again to be in the habit of making friends with neighbors on trains, buses, and airplanes. There's a heightened delight meeting strangers in liminal spaces: in the interstices of life's patterns there's open space for exploration, novelty, and  unexpected connection.

Dispositionally, I'm basically inclined to like everyone, though through encrustation in bitterness and self-involved anguish over years I became more of a misanthrope in adolescence and into my early twenties. Glad to be out the other side and seeing sunshine, again.

text:
I met a beautiful young man,
Admired wherever he is.

Restless, he revealed,
He's afraid to die,
Even more to live.

Get out of your head,
Someone said,
Left.

I told him he could do both:
Dying in life,
Living in death,
Leaping clear of both.

Maybe he will,
For time to tell.

text:
"Another reservation about authenticity, and by association meditative practices interpreted through its lens, is what we might call a socioethical critique. The sociologist Christopher Lasch famously articulated this critique in his phrase “the culture of narcissism” (1991 [1979]). The worry is that, aside from whether it is possible to have the kind of self-transparency, certainty, and independent autonomy to which the ethic of authenticity aspires, the very aspiration is too self-centered and has deleterious effects on society. It is adjacent to a pervasive emphasis on self-fulfillment that implies, in his view, a degraded ability to recognize ethical demands external to efforts at one’s own self-actualization. It might weaken social bonds and diminish empathy if it simply reduces the role of others to instrumental means to one’s own self-fulfilling ends. This may contribute to disengagement with social and political realities and might even be deleterious to democracy itself, which presupposes citizen engagement. It also may lead to a kind of atomism and social isolation that sociologists argue are increasing in postindustrial societies (Putnam 2000). Finally, it rests on what Robert Bellah has called “ontological individualism,” the conviction that human beings are fundamentally independent and separate beings to whom sociality is essentially compromising, unnatural, and alienating from one’s true nature (Bellah et al. 1985, Trilling 1972, Guignon 2004)."

- Rethinking Meditation, David L. McMahan

text: "Excised from the comprehensive ethical and philosophical worldview of Buddhism, it might foster a kind of liberalism of neutrality that carries no particular values except what is decided by individual practitioners in the fulfillment of their goals. At its most banal, mindfulness might become simply another instrument of narcissism, an accoutrement of developing a superficial, atomistic identity, an aid to “lifestyle enhancement” rather than a practice of deep critical self-inquiry. Moreover, discovering oneself in the age of social media is almost inevitably mediated through commercial interests that monetize and commodify countless tools for constructing identity, from clothes to gadgets to, indeed, mindfulness itself. Or, a parallel concern, mindfulness may create a passive, spectatorial mode of disengagement with broader social and political realities beyond the self."

text: "A more viable ideal of authenticity, then, involves committing to a notion of the good that is not simply one’s own self-discovered possession but a matter of collective questioning and democratic negotiation. In a similar vein, Charles Guignon suggests that authenticity need not be synonymous with self-absorption but is, to the contrary, only possible in a “free society,” and thus it carries with it obligations to sustain and nurture that society’s well-being. This can only happen through being clear and committed to one’s desires and convictions in the democratic arena (Guignon 2004)."

text:
"With no reference for the body, objects, and phenomena,
since everything is even in the open expanse of space,
there are no so-called inner phenomena, as there is no fixation with self.

When looking there at the objects of outer phenomena,
all is unobscured, insubstantial, and transparent.
Like blurred vision that is free from aiming,
whatever appears, whatever is heard, whatever is recalled,
whatever is experienced and whatever is felt are not the same as before.
What is this? The phenomena of a madman,
or like in a dream just laughing at myself?"

- Longchenpa, Jewel Treasure of the Dharmadhatu

text:
"If at this point your spirit and morale slacken, all the more rely on this aspiration. If faith in the heart is shallow and weak, all the more rely on this aspiration. If obstacles are many, all the more rely on this aspiration. If karmic habits are manifold, all the more rely on this aspiration. If intelligent and clever, all the more rely on this aspiration. If stupid and dull, all the more rely on this aspiration. If seeing into the True Nature becomes fully clear, all the more rely on this aspiration. If insight and function become fully free, all the more rely on this aspiration. Right from the beginning, from the first aspiration of the heart to the final end, there is no time when you do not rely on the strength of this aspiration.
...
Reciting the Four Great Vows, directing them from the mouth outwards, and inwardly ever holding them in the heart, invoking them as a prayer day by day and continuously pondering them, then just like a wondrous scent or an old strange custom, or like fine mist that yet drenches one's clothes, or as the smell of incense pervades and clings, so the awareness of Buddhas and patriarchs will ripen of itself and, benefiting oneself and others, everything will be accomplished."

- Torei

text:
"Subdue the enemy of hatred with the sword of love.
Protect the family of the denizens of the six realms with skillful care.
Reap from the field of confidence the harvest of experience and realization.
Accomplishing your life’s work, chant the six-beat mantra.

Burn the corpse of attachment to reality in the fire of nonattachment.
Through the essence of Dharma, conduct the funeral rites for appearances.
Dedicate the merit you gain from the smoke offering for their next life.
Having dedicated all of your virtue to these dead, chant the six-beat mantra.

Place your son—confidence—at the Dharma door of your practice.
Give the house of appearances to your son-in-law— renunciation.
Marry your daughter—care—to the bridegroom of the triple world.
Having done your duty to the living, chant the six-beat mantra."

- Patrul Rinpoche

text:
"...nonself functions in particular ways when drawn into the orbit of late-modern secular subjectivity and its chaotic liquidity, media saturation, instability, disembeddedness, and fragmentation—not to mention the nostalgia for the stable, self-responsible agent of the Enlightenment.
...
If we have never had a coherent, stable, permanent self to begin with, then attempting to construct one is not only unnecessary but futile. Following this logic, it is better to recognize the fluid, malleable nature of consciousness, be aware of how various “selves” rise and fall depending on diverse causes and conditions, and learn to skillfully guide the process. This approach might serve to mitigate the anxiety of trying to anchor a stable sense of selfhood amid the whirlwind of ever-changing conditions of the late-modern period. If the bad news for the modern autonomous self is that it was a fiction to begin with—something that philosophy, psychology, neuroscience, and social science increasingly agree on—the good news is that a rich, meaningful, and ethical life is available in its absence. If the fragmenting forces of late modernity have shattered the illusion of a fixed self, anātman provides a way of rethinking subjectivity in its absence."

...
If we have never had a coherent, stable, permanent self to begin with, then attempting to construct one is not only unnecessary but futile. Following this logic, it is better to recognize the fluid, malleable nature of consciousness, be aware of how various “selves” rise and fall depending on diverse causes and conditions, and learn to skillfully guide the process. This approach might serve to mitigate the anxiety of trying to anchor a stable sense of selfhood amid the whirlwind of ever-changing conditions of the late-modern period. If the bad news for the modern autonomous self is that it was a fiction to begin with—something that philosophy, psychology, neuroscience, and social science increasingly agree on—the good news is that a rich, meaningful, and ethical life is available in its absence. If the fragmenting forces of late modernity have shattered the illusion of a fixed self, anātman provides a way of rethinking subjectivity in its absence."

text:
Rigdzin Jigme Lingpa:

"Even if he has dwelt for many millions of years
In mountains and valleys full of poisonous snakes five hundred miles away,
A bodhisattva who does not understand solitude,
Gaining only greater pride, will remain an ordinary being.

So, having understood the essential point of bringing whatever arises onto the path, the accomplished one who thinks of his body as a retreat hut does not rely on the number of months and years he has spent in retreat, since his is not a path that gives great import to the accomplishment of remaining in solitude in itself. Having developed to his full measure in this very life, he strives to maintain the flow of the unelaborated natural state. Though good or bad thoughts and emotions arise, he does not engage in any activities such as applying the moxibustion of the antidote to the object of rejection or applying the patch of naked apprehension that is the idea: “Whatever good or bad thoughts and emotions arise, they are just thoughts and emotions.” Throughout day and night he crosses over without interval to a state of non-elaboration, as unconcerned as an old man watching a child at play."

text:
"This attitude toward the Dhamma parallels what ancient cultures called "warrior knowledge" — the knowledge that comes from developing skills in difficult situations — as opposed to the "scribe knowledge" that people sitting in relative security and ease can write down in words. Of course, warriors need to use words in their training, but they view a text as authoritative only if its teachings are borne out in practice. The Canon itself encourages this attitude when it quotes the Buddha as teaching his aunt, "As for the teachings of which you may know, 'These teachings lead to dispassion, not to passion; to being unfettered, not to being fettered; to divesting, not to accumulating; to modesty, not to self-aggrandizement; to contentment, not to discontent; to seclusion, not to entanglement; to aroused persistence, not to laziness; to being unburdensome, not to being burdensome': You may definitely hold, 'This is the Dhamma, this is the Vinaya, this is the Teacher's instruction.'"
...
The survival of Buddhism and the survival of the Dhamma are two different things... Of course, people have always been free to engage in Buddhist traditions in whatever way they like, but those who have benefited most from that engagement are those who, instead of reshaping Buddhism to fit their preferences, reshape themselves to fit in with the customs and traditions of the noble ones. To find these customs isn't easy, given the bewildering variety of traditions that Buddhists have spawned over the centuries. To test them, each individual is thrown back on his or her own powers of relentless honesty, integrity, and discernment."

- Thanissaro Bhikku

text:
Teaching this first retreat focused on the jhanas, I felt it important to present them within the scope of their original context as best as possible, as an integral part of the noble eightfold path leading to awakening as understood within the early Buddhist Canon (with reference and connection to later Buddhist tradition at the end). A retreatant remarked I had my 'Theravada hat' on.😄

Though I'm a poor representative, I attempted to faithfully pass on what I've received from my own teachers and what the suttas themselves say. I had intended to record and release the talks from the retreat, but the AV setup didn't come together in time. text:
Percipient of nibbana:

“Friend Sāriputta, could a monk have an attainment of concentration such that he would neither be percipient of earth with regard to earth, nor of water with regard to water, nor of fire… wind… the dimension of the infinitude of space… the dimension of the infinitude of consciousness… the dimension of nothingness… the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception… this world… nor of the next world with regard to the next world, and yet he would still be percipient?”

“Yes, friend Ānanda, he could.…”
...
“Once, friend Ānanda, when I was staying right here near Sāvatthī in the Grove of the Blind, I reached concentration in such a way that I was neither percipient of earth with regard to earth…nor of the next world with regard to the next world, and yet I was still percipient.”

“But what, friend Sāriputta, were you percipient of at that time?”

“‘The cessation of becoming—unbinding—the cessation of becoming—unbinding’: One perception arose in me, friend Ānanda, as another perception ceased. Just as in a blazing woodchip fire, one flame arises as another flame ceases, even so, ‘The cessation of becoming—unbinding—the cessation of becoming—unbinding’: One perception arose in me as another one ceased. I was percipient at that time of ‘The cessation of becoming—unbinding.’”

text:
"Burn up what’s before,
and have nothing for after.
If you don’t grasp
at what’s in between,
you will go about, calm.

For whom, in name-&-form,
in every way,
there’s no sense of mine,
and who doesn’t grieve
over what is not:
He, in the world,
isn’t defeated,
suffers no loss."

text:
This was mostly about intent, determination, and aspiration, but practically, if you'd like to swiftly advance in whatever method of practice you are engaged in, the time-tested formula is not at all complicated, but few are actually willing to apply it:

- Set your intent. Become clear within yourself what you aim to accomplish and why.
- Minimize any and all distractions. Put aside all unnecessary activities of body, speech, and mind. Practice wholeheartedly and with unstinting resolve in seclusion or else in the company of noble friends.
- Meet with reliable guides as needed (which for most of us is regularly) to correct straying from the path.
- Don't concern yourself with whether you are far or near from the goal, only return continually to this moment of practice until meditation is unceasing and the spontaneous, seamless wisdom of awakening encompasses all activity.

text:
"When you have gained realization in this way,
The whole phenomenal world is the book of oral instructions and the real mandala.
On the multicolored parchment of appearances,
Awareness, the bamboo pen of self-existing wisdom,
Inscribes the letters of nonfixation that are groundless and primordially free.
This is read as the nonduality of appearance and emptiness.

On this spontaneously perfect mandala of the three-thousandfold universe,
Is sprinkled the water of naturalness
The pathways are the natural lines of the design
And your footsteps are the drawings in colored powder.
Your own appearing yet empty body is the form of the divine yidam.
Your speech, resounding and yet empty, is its vajra recitation,
while your naturally freed and unfixated thoughts are the mind of the deity.

The movements of your arms and legs are mudras
And eating and drinking is the dharmata offering.
All appearance of form is the body of the deity.
All sounds and speech are musical offerings.
Beyond keeping and breaking, this is the naturally fulfilled samaya."

text:
From Longchenpa's death poem, Immaculate Radiance:

"Now it is time to go, like a traveller upon the way.
The delight I have in death is far, far greater than
The delight of traders who find their fortune at sea,
Or the lords of devas who prove victorious in battle,
Or adepts who find ecstasy in dhyāna concentration.

Now, I, Pema Ledrel Tsal, shall remain no longer,
But go to capture the deathless state of great bliss.

Life is finished, karma complete, prayer has no more power.
All worldly things are done with, this life’s show has ended.
Soon I will recognize in a single instant the extensive visions
Of the bardo realms as manifestations of my awareness
And take my place forevermore in the ground primordial.

Let my own fortune inspire happiness in the minds of others,
And let this illusory life realize the benefit of liberation’s isle."

text:
Join me online February 13-22 for an immersion cultivating continuous samadhi and exploring the themes of emptiness and Buddha-nature.

The content of this retreat is pitched at intermediate and advanced practitioners, though beginners are also welcome to apply and attend. I’ll be reviewing foundational jhana practice as I learned from Leigh Brasington, though the bulk of the group instruction will be focused on insight into the themes of emptiness and recognition of our Buddha-nature. I suggest you read and review Leigh’s excellent manual, Right Concentration, before attending. If you’re less familiar with jhana and would like to focus on developing fluency for the duration of the retreat, that’s also welcome. The talks will be recorded and may be more useful for you later on.

Depending on how many people register, 1:1 practice interviews will be daily or every other day.

Though I recommend setting aside all activities besides practice for the duration of the retreat if possible, if you’re able to practice at least 4 hours a day or more while working or attending to other necessary responsibilities I want to facilitate your commitment to practice and participation.

Further details, including a full syllabus, will be forthcoming once I complete my current period of monastic training in mid-January.

If you would like to attend, please apply through the form linked below and I’ll be in touch. Responses will be delayed for now as I presently only access the internet irregularly.

This retreat is offered on the basis of your generosity (dāna). A donation of $250-500 is suggested, though any amount is appreciated and no one will be turned away for inability to contribute.

text:
Some of my wandering thoughts throughout this first half of Ango have been mulling how to practically structure my own life going forwards to sustain and support this level of commitment to practice, and offer that same structure and support for others who are also called to resolve their life's dis-ease at the root.

Will have to wait until I'm out and about come mid-January to properly give it form, but looking to engage community-building around awakening-oriented intensive lay practice involving intermittent adherence to the eight precepts and basic social accountability mechanisms reinforcing prioritizing the path, to start.

Samvega Sangha, stay tuned.

text:
Relatedly, this may be helpful to share: I used to entertain an idle fantasy that if I exerted myself strenuously enough in meditation for a sufficient duration I would eventually attain an awakening so transformative as to require no further deliberate practice, and I would be free to engage life without any cumbersome commitment to yogic discipline.

Yes, you can attain unshakeable seeing of your mind's nature, but this does not obviate the necessity and benefit of further training. Residual habituated delusion, embedded and embodied, continues to distort functioning without a constancy of continued development of samadhi applied to cutting through what remains.

Retrospectively, now again in a full-time training container, with the state of my body, heart, and mind clearly reflected in all their flaws in the austere daily regimen here, it's obvious the extent to which I had been comfortably complacent, and how my many continued errors spring therefrom.

It's also simply a fact I've observed again and again that this mammalian nervous system is happiest, healthiest, and most helpful on a healthy diet of sustained meditative absorption and seclusion from the chronic overstimulation and trivial entertainments of contemporary culture.

I'm exceptionally fortunate to have in my own teachers examples of awakened integrity navigating this absurd kaleidoscope of 21st century post-modernity, and I feel invigorated to follow in my own fumbling way and encourage others in the same endeavor.

What joy!
What joy!

Verse of Purification

All my past and harmful karma, born from beginningless greed, hate, and delusion, through body, speech, and mind, I now fully avow.

The Four Great Vows

Sentient beings are boundless: I vow to liberate them. 
Afflictions are endless: I vow to cut them off. 
Dharma gates are immeasurable: I vow to practice them. 
The Buddha way is unsurpassable: I vow to attain it.

text:
"I see them,
in the world, floundering around,
people immersed in craving
for states of becoming.

Base people moan in the mouth of death,
their craving, for states of becoming & not-,
unallayed.

See them,
floundering in their sense of mine,
like fish in the puddles
of a dried-up stream—
and, seeing this,
live with no mine,
not forming attachment
for states of becoming.

Subdue desire
for both sides,
comprehending sensory contact,
with no greed.

Doing nothing for which
he himself
would rebuke himself,
the enlightened person doesn’t adhere
to what’s seen,
to what’s heard.

Comprehending perception,
he’d cross over the flood—
the sage not stuck
on possessions.

Then, with arrow removed,
living heedfully, he longs for neither—
this world,
the next."

in the flood of great danger—birth—

there is no other.
That’s unbinding, I tell you,
the total ending of aging & death."

text:
More errant thoughts emerging from retreat:

Immersing again in more rigorous and conservative religious training makes me both more appreciative of the traditions I have and will continue to train in and also more relaxed and confident in the ways in which I diverge as a practitioner and teacher.

Maintaining with total fidelity inherited pedagogical forms leading to liberation in this life is an incalculable service, but not precisely what I'm called to. With respect and permission, rather, reweaving the threads of lineage to bridge with natural science and my native culture is the play.

I hope for a thriving, vital blossoming of Buddhadharma here, live and laser-focused on awakening with all the tools, traditional and experimental, available. Only time and trial will tell what will be of lasting benefit.

Faithful to essence, experimenting with form.

text:
"As mind has no task in primordial purity, the exhaustion of phenomena,
There is uninterrupted openness, a spacious and euphoric experience,
And everything is perfectly even and uniform, like broad, expansive sky.
In this phenomenal exhaustion, intellect also disappears—how delightful!

All external entities are like magical illusions or dreams,
The moon in water, hallucinations, or cities in the clouds—
Clearly apparent yet unreal, the very forms of emptiness.
How happy I shall be to see them from this day forth!"

- Longchenpa

text:
"Listening to the teachings—you’ve already heard hundreds of teachings,
But when you haven’t grasped the meaning of even one teaching,
What’s the point of more listening?
...
Giving high-sounding lectures
Doesn’t do your mind-stream any good.
The path of analytical reasoning is precise and acute—
But it’s just more delusion, good-for-nothing goat-shit.
The oral instructions are very profound
But not if you don’t put them into practice.

Reading over and over those dharma texts
That just occupy your mind and make your eyes sore—
Forget about it!
...
Giving teachings on meditation texts
Without yourself having
Gained actual experience through practice,
Is like reciting a dance-manual out loud
And thinking that’s the same as actually dancing.

People may be listening to you with devotion,
But it just isn’t the real thing.

Sooner or later, when your own actions
Contradict the teachings, you’ll feel ashamed.

Just mouthing the words,
Giving dharma explanations that sound so eloquent—
Forget about it!
...
If you let go of everything—
Everything, everything—
That’s the real point!"

- Advice from Me to Myself, Patrul Rinpoche

There is something here now, true of and constituting our every experience, which if recognized would radically refigure all your life hereafter.

Unarisen, unceasing, groundlessly present.

It is labeled differently:
Your original face,
The nature of mind,
Dharmakaya,
Sugatagarbha.

A union of empty cognizance apparent as manifest diversity from which we have never strayed.

Complete!

I have nothing to add,
But see for yourself!

text:
When I set out intent to accomplish the path and win freedom from the rounds of my misery, though I may have mouthed contrary, at heart I took the endeavor up as a solitary enterprise, taken as I was by the heroic narratives, often marked by great solitude and disaffection with company, of great yogins gone by. Even as I trained in communal environments, I kept myself shuttered, nose-down and focused on my own practice, my path, my liberation. Not that there's nothing good there, but it's apparent now also how much it subtly reinforced the very dualistic discrimination I desired to discard. Long enculturation in our individualistic frame no doubt fanned the flame, likewise.

Looking back, I was alternatingly diffident and arrogant.

Living again these past three months with fellows in the contemplative life, working closely with a teacher, it's impressed upon me anew the foundation of human relationship upon which all transmission of the Dharma down to us has come. How awakening must not only be attained but lived in each act, connected with all beings, concretely in daily affairs. Cooking, cleaning, caring for space, caring for time, caring for each other.

No one can do it for anyone else; each must arrive through their own efforts, yet no one arrives alone.

text:
Waiting for a bus so here's a stream of consciousness while sitting:

3 months gone, feels more a year, come back anytime Roshi says, a home away in the heart of it all, home's the heart empty of all, Prajnaparamita-Hridaya Sutra, universe in a drop, chanted felt thrumming through the bones, Four Vows vibrate every cell, visions radiating each syllable ever outward, endless, endless, still feel the congestion clogging up, this silly body subject to sickness unto aging and death, deathless doesn't obstruct the consequences of being born now, does it, stare straight out through the window into the bare, pale winter's day and all thoughts stop, as do I, mind motionless as mind-itself rests radiant, funny no-thing I am, we are.

text:
Brahmavihara Weekend Immersion
January 31 - February 1

Join me for a weekend cultivating the divine abodes taught in Buddhadharma: metta (loving-kindness), karuna (compassion), mudita (sympathetic joy), and upekkha (equanimity). If a suitable venue can be found in time, you’re welcome to join in-person in NYC and it will be a hybrid event, otherwise online only.

1:1 practice interviews will be available in addition to the guided meditations and talks.

SCHEDULE (all times US Eastern):

Saturday, March 31
10:00: Welcome and Opening Talk
11:00: Guided Metta Practice
12:00 - : Independent Practice
13:00 - 15:00: 1:1 Interviews
15:30 - 16:30: Guided Karuna Practice
17:00 - 19:00: 1:1 Interviews
19:30 - 21:00 Talk, Q&A, Guided Mudita Practice

Sunday, February 1
10:00: Chanting, Guided Upekkha Practice
11:00 - : Independent Practice
11:30 - 14:00 1:1 Interviews
14:30: Closing and Farewell

If you would like to attend, see the link below and fill out the form.

This short retreat is offered on the basis of your generosity (Dāna). A donation of $100-150 is suggested, though any amount is appreciated and no one will be turned away for inability to contribute.

May you and all beings likewise abide in love, compassion, joy, and profound equanimity!

text: "The true, highest teaching according to Zen, therefore, is neither sutric nor tantric; it is our own intrinsic wisdom, which is not bound by sutra or tantra. The supreme meditation method is not any technique of manipulating thoughts, being “mindful,” or “staying in the present moment,” but rather to turn around and directly recognize the luminous, boundless nature of one’s own mind. The true esoteric Buddhist teachings are not secretly transmitted mantras and hidden practices, but rather the direct recognition of one’s true nature by which the point of all Buddhist practices is clearly seen. The highest transmission and initiation one may receive is just this actual awakening, no matter how it is accomplished. The supreme vehicle is the ascending path—free from all limitations—that only awakening reveals."

text:
"Emaho! How wondrous!
When distilled into a single ultimate point, it is as follows:

Look, at all times, into this store of precious treasure,
The natural Great Perfection, the sovereign nature of mind,
Which is not created by anyone but spontaneously arisen from the first.

...

The primordial state defies expression, is groundless and unsourced.
There is no acceptance, rejection, striving, hope, fear or partiality.
When remaining in such a state, all is dharmakāya,
The effortless self-liberation of things left as they are.

When you see reality as it is, there is no foundation or source.
In seeing the sole solution, there is not a scintilla of meditation.
For one who is without conceit, there is no formulaic conduct.

...

There is no time to delay. Practise diligently, child!
Let everything be excellent and auspicious! Virtue!"

- Longchenpa

text:
Greatly looking forward to facilitating this upcoming retreat!

Could accommodate up to 4 more participants, if there's interest. It's a likely overly ambitious slate of topics covered in so short a time, but 1:1 and in Q&A I'll of course be meeting everyone where they're at, and the rest, I hope, will unfold in time.

Syllabus and schedule in outline:

Syllabus

Friday:
In brief and for review:
- Four Noble Truths
- Five Recollections
- Four Thoughts that Turn the Mind
- Refuge
- Eightfold Noble Path and the function of Sila/Precepts

Saturday:
- Anapanasati
- Metta-bhavana
- Five Hindrances
- Access Concentration
- First Jhana

Sunday:
- First Four Jhanas

Monday:
- Open Q&A

Tuesday:
- Formless Jhanas

Wednesday:
- An Introduction to Emptiness

Thursday:
- Emptiness Continued

Friday:
- Tathagatagarbha and The Nature of Mind

Saturday:
- Essence, Nature, and Compassion

Sunday:
- Parting thoughts and wishes

Schedule (All times US Eastern)

Friday 13
20:00: Orientation and Opening Talk
21:00: Guided Practice

Saturday 14 - Saturday 21
9:00: Guided Practice
10:00: Instruction - Q&A
11:00 - 17:00: Independent Practice & Interviews
17:00: Guided Practice
18:00 - 20:00: Independent Practice
20:00: Talk - Q&A
21:00: Guided Practice

Sunday 22
9:00: Guided Practice
10:00: Closing Talk and Farewell

"Once it breaks up, the true dignity of each and every one is revealed; beauty beyond compare is manifest everywhere. Then you’ll know what needs to be done in this world. You’ll not always succeed; I often fail. But you’ll know what needs to be done, and you’ll be able to give all to it and learn in the process. Others will not always understand; the desired result may not come. But that, too, will be the Dharma in its fullness."

- Jeff Shore

text: "What is a sutra? A sutra is a record of a meeting in which the student’s mind and the teacher’s mind come together. For such a meeting to come about, both teacher and student must drop their respective worlds and meet in an open space that neither of them defines. Only in that mystery does true communication and true learning take place. A sutra is a record of what happens in that space."

text:
In-person and online sangha-building around this intention will have to wait until I'm less busy and settled in one place (August earliest), but for myself, and with friends holding me accountable, I'm committing to 3 hours formal practice daily undergirding, when I'm not traveling, a consistent schedule running from Sunday afternoon through Friday afternoon, and in that window in addition to adhering to the usual 5 lay precepts I'm abstaining from entertainment. I find when I'm sitting that much I don't care much for conventional entertainment anyway, but wasting time that way has been a major stumbling block in the past.

This kind of tight regularity is a reliable condition for my thriving, and it's high time I take what I've learned through years of monastic training and carry it through day-to-day.

Starting tomorrow.

text:
"We never say you must leave a normal layperson’s life behind. Yet the other side of this coin is that you must indeed do a great deal of something within your so-called normal life. That something includes formal daily practice, done according to the instructions received from your teacher. This is indispensable. In fact, you will need to do enough of this practice that to other people your normal life might not seem so normal at all! I want to stress again: we do not make a great distinction between lay or ordained. That does not matter. What matters is the question, “How are you spending the time during each of your days?”

- Meido Roshi, The Rinzai Zen Way

On the subject and since someone asked recently: a rough consensus among teachers I rely on and look up to have concluded from their own decades of practice and teaching experience that working up to 2 hours/day or more of formal practice + intentional round-the-clock integration in all activities + retreat when possible gives best results if sincerely aiming at awakening as a layperson.

Go for it!

text:
Sharing, from a talk given at Korinji on vows, compassion, and ethical conduct in difficult times. Open to all on their Patreon, linked below.

"The Buddha spoke directly about unjust and corrupt kings. He stated clearly that governance grounded in greed and self-interest leads to suffering and social collapse, and that only moral rule could ensure the well-being of a society. So, we pray for the dissolving of negative conditions, and the transforming of people’s minds, such that a just, moral, and ethically grounded governance can arise. Again, we wish for the transformation – or at worst, the removal from power – of immoral leaders, rather than for harm to befall anyone...

Compassion does not mean standing in a position of neutrality between oppressor and oppressed; it means standing firmly for the cessation of harm, which does not require hatred, but does not permit cruelty. Thus, if we are to model wisdom and compassion in our actions of body, speech, and mind, it seems to me unavoidable that there are times when body, speech, and mind must each be used fearlessly and loudly. And when we do so, let it be with acts and words of reason, modeling not only strength but kindness. Without expressing hatred, we may speak the truth while refusing to denigrate or dehumanize even those who have chosen to dehumanize themselves through their own hatred and ignorance."

text: "Because consciousness is not established anywhere, it does not increase; because of not increasing, it is not active anywhere; because of not being active anywhere, it is stable; because of being stable, it is contented; because of being contented, it is freed; because of being freed, there is no clinging to anything in the whole world; because of not clinging to anything, there is no being attached to anything. Because of not being attached to anything, one personally realizes Nirvana."

text:
🙂Good book.

"Whose pasture is emptiness
And signless concentration
Their track is hard to trace,
Like that of birds in the sky."

The nun Khemā discoursing with a monarch:

"Great King, a Tathāgata is freed from being reckoned in terms of bodily form...feeling...perception...volitional formations...consciousness, being deep, immeasurable, and difficult to fathom, just like the great ocean.

‘A Tathāgata exists after death’ does not apply, ‘a Tathāgata does not exist after death’ also does not apply, ‘a Tathāgata exists and does not exist after death’ also does not apply, ‘a Tathāgata neither exists nor does not exist after death’ also does not apply.”

Bhikku Ñāṇananda:
 
"If, for instance, a vortex in the ocean comes to cease, can one ask where the vortex has gone? It will be like asking where the extinguished fire has gone. One might say that the vortex has “joined” the ocean. But that, too, would not be a proper statement to make. From the very outset what in fact was there was the great ocean, so one cannot say that the vortex has gone somewhere, nor can one say that it is not gone...

The cessation of suffering could therefore be compared to the cessation of the vortex, leaving only the great ocean as it is... It is where a vortex ceases that the great ocean prevails unhindered. To give up the limitations of a vortex, is to inherit the limitless ocean... With the cessation of a vortex, the attention of one who has been looking at it turns towards the depth, immeasurability and boundlessness of the great ocean."

text:
At first one can confuse thoughts for reality.
Then cling to emotions as truth.
Eventually clutching apparently bare sensation as secure.
Or, abiding as unfocused awareness, settle in stupor.
And when, if all this ceases, make something of it and set it against appearance.
Oops.

text:
📢Happy to announce @algekalipso and I will be jointly presenting @thesfcommons about classical and contemporary perspectives on consciousness and awakening the evening of Friday, February 27!

I'll provide a brief overview of traditional Buddhist views from the early tradition through developments in the Mahayana and Vajrayana, and lead a short meditation, before handing it off to Andres to share from QRI's lens on consciousness, suffering, and exotic extremes of well-being before opening it up for discussion and Q&A.

We'd be delighted for you to join us!
Full info and registration below.🙂

text:
@thesfcommons Saturday, February 28:

Who are we?

I invite you to join for an afternoon's guided exploration of meditative inquiry into fundamental questions of identity and consciousness: who, what, and where are we?

​Through group, dyad, and solo meditations, the aim of this workshop is to directly investigate the nature of mind.

​Schedule:

​2PM: Welcome, Intro, and Guided Practice

​3PM: Dyadic Inquiry

​4PM: Solo Inquiry

​5PM: Guided Practice, Q&A

6PM: Close

​During the solo section I'll be available for 1:1s regarding practice.

Registration and event page below!🙂

text:
"The nature of awakened mind, neither clung to nor beheld,
Yet it appears to all as something to be seen.
Undisturbed by anything, it cannot be dissected.
Such natural mind, on nothing does it dwell.
This knowing is the nature of awakened mind.
However modified, this space is unaffected,
And rigpa need not seek this basic space.
Mind-essence’s pure reality, like space,
Is uncontrived, devoid of true existence.
The training, I have realized, is simply this.
When this body made of matter falls apart,
Samantabhadra's heart will shine within me."

- Buddhamati, excerpted from Wellsprings of the Great Perfection

text:
These tenuous threads:
Skein of sinew and sign,
All my relations:

Revolving without ground,
An untethered trapeze,
The net untangled!

See without looking,
Hear without locating,
Feel without latching,
Love lacking longing rings round,
Sings loud,
Offers up,
Setting down,
Embracing all.

text:
Pictured, the adoration of Buddha's empty throne.

From the Dhātu-vibhaṅga Sutta:

"‘I am’ is a supposition. ‘I am this’ is a supposition. ‘I shall be’ is a supposition. ‘I shall not be’… ‘I shall be possessed of form’ … ‘I shall not be possessed of form’ … ‘I shall be percipient’ … ‘I shall not be percipient’ … ‘I shall be neither percipient nor non-percipient’ is a supposition. Supposition is a disease, supposition is a cancer, supposition is an arrow. By going beyond all supposition, he is called a sage at peace.

And further, a sage at peace is not born, does not age, does not die, is unagitated, and is free from longing. He has nothing whereby he would be born. Not being born, will he age? Not aging, will he die? Not dying, will he be agitated? Not being agitated, for what will he long? It was in reference to this that it was said, ‘He has been stilled where the currents of supposition do not flow. And when the currents of supposition do not flow, he is said to be a sage at peace.’"

text:
In the airport en route to SF.🙂

For this Saturday's workshop @thesfcommons , I wanted to offer an engaging and tractable translation, a taster, of the kind of inquiry I've come to love common to Zen and Dzogchen.

I'll be doing my best to help participants embody and explore that unknowing which opens the heart and relases the mind from its usual bonds of identity and habit.

As with every event, my hope is something in the air will spark and spur everyone on to greater depth and presence in their own lives.

Come along if intrigued!

Departing NYC, reflecting on the last month, happy with the retreat concluded Sunday, which for me was a kind of culmination attempting to present in outline the entirety of path as I've practiced and understand it, representing the scope and diversity of my own influences.🥲

As well, delighted by the friends reunited with and made during my stay, and looking forward to moving in properly come summertime and shifting more efforts to sangha-building in Brooklyn.

text:
"Our immediate perception is simply distorted. When the distortion is cleared, everything within one's personal experience is seen as purity. Ordinary beings cannot perceive this purity, but by becoming an accomplished yogi yourself, you will see this basic purity. This is the difference between personal perception and the perception of others: you can see other beings as pure because they are already pure; but due to their obscurations, they themselves don't perceive this purity. 
...
The mundane experience of phenomena is called distorted perception, the confused perception of sentient beings. In the experience of someone who has pure perception, a house will be a celestial palace. In the celestial palace, there is no experience of earth, water, fire, or wind. Everything is rainbow light. How amazing!"

- Tulku Urgyen Rinpoche

text:
SN 4:15:

“When embraced,
the rod of violence
breeds danger & fear:
Look at people in strife.
I will tell how
I experienced
terror:
Seeing people floundering
like fish in small puddles,
competing with one another—
as I saw this,
fear came into me.
The world was entirely
without substance.
All the directions
were knocked out of line.
Wanting a haven for myself,
I saw nothing that wasn’t laid claim to.
Seeing nothing in the end
but competition,
I felt discontent.
And then I saw
an arrow here,
so very hard to see,
embedded in the heart.
Overcome by this arrow
you run in all directions.
But simply on pulling it out
you don’t run,
you don’t sink."

text:
Two trends with some people I see, and suggestion:

Wanting guidance on what to want in terms of practice and path. Disoriented by the spiritual smorgasbord, there's a hesitancy to take more than the first bite of any option, worried about getting it right. Holding open space without collapsing the uncertainty for them while offering a few places to start, encouraging they exercise their own discernment seems best, here.

Related to the first, when there is some more direction, still wrestling with the same restlessness, toggling and cycling through different methods and developmental frameworks, looking for a silver bullet which will optimally deliver the present soteriological and/or psychological MacGuffin of choice.

It's well and good to have a map and compass for the journey, even to know, use, and compare across different cartographical conventions and navigational devices. Once you've got your bearings, however, all there is to do is fold up, stow away the map, put the compass in your pocket, and walk, enjoying the view along the way.

Two trends with some people I see, and suggestion:

Wanting guidance on what to want in terms of practice and path. Disoriented by the spiritual smorgasbord, there's a hesitancy to take more than the first bite of any option, worried about getting it right. Holding open space without collapsing the uncertainty for them while offering a few places to start, encouraging they exercise their own discernment seems best, here.

Related to the first, when there is some more direction, still wrestling with the same restlessness, toggling and cycling through different methods and developmental frameworks, looking for a silver bullet which will optimally deliver the present soteriological and/or psychological MacGuffin of choice.

It's well and good to have a map and compass for the journey, even to know, use, and compare across different cartographical conventions and navigational devices. Once you've got your bearings, however, all there is to do is fold up, stow away the map, put the compass in your pocket, and walk, enjoying the view along the way.

text:
While it's still fresh and before heading back into the second leg of retreat this month on Wednesday, I want to shout out Dhamma Dena, the retreat center I just came from, where Leigh was teaching. Sweet note, that it was there that Leigh went on his very first meditation retreat with Ayya Khema decades ago.
What a rare and precious thing, an oasis of Dhamma in the desert run solely on dana.
Learning a little of the story of the founder, Ruth Denison, her hardship, dedication to service, and inspiration to others, and speaking with those who knew her, how wonderful her vision lives on! Apparently Shinzen lived there for a year back in the 80s. Small world!
Anyway, a beautiful place, highly recommended you check it out if interested in solo retreats, other events, or have some way to contribute to the good work being done. Link to their website and a little about Ruth below.

text:
When Void first brought the idea up to me, and hearing how the vision has unfolded since, I'm delighted to see it's happening! I'm hopeful the offerings will bridge a gap in the contemporary landscape of American spirituality.

We're fortunate now that if you are seriously interested in pursuing and realizing classical awakening, and you know where to look, there are now more accessible teachers and resources to support you than perhaps at any other time.

Simultaneously, there's a growing crisis of the relative, socialized self amidst rapidly changing social and economic conditions, the educational and relational needs and appropriate functioning of which are not always, and honestly not often, well tended to in many spiritual communities and pedagogies.

May we, all of us, train to wake up and and show up as mature, confident, capable, and fully flourishing individuals.

I wish VV every success, the work to benefit many, and I'm glad to be participating on the board.
❤️

text:
Many early childhood memories surfacing these past weeks, feeling the presence and love of God as a boy, the hymns I would sing, the prayers recited, the holy spirit moving as and through the vivid, transparent world of light and song. As I've gone further with Buddhadharma I appreciate only more my roots.❤️

All shall be well, and all shall be well, and all manner of thing shall be well.
Peace be with you!

Lord, make me an instrument of your peace.
Where there is hatred, let me bring love.
Where there is offence, let me bring pardon.
Where there is discord, let me bring union.
Where there is error, let me bring truth.
Where there is doubt, let me bring faith.
Where there is despair, let me bring hope.
Where there is darkness, let me bring your light.
Where there is sadness, let me bring joy.
O Lord, grant that I may not so much seek
to be consoled as to console,
to be understood as to understand,
to be loved as to love,
for it is in giving that one receives,
it is in self-forgetting that one finds,
it is in forgiving that one is forgiven,
it is in dying that one awakens to eternal life.

text:
Can't speak for VV, but:

I came to practice with a dysfunctional ego-structure and sought for years to transcend my conventional functional and emotional deficits by more samatha-vipassana, and it was mostly bypass. In retrospect, I would have been better helped by earlier developing a richer understanding and working with my surface psychology in tandem with the traditional training in sila, samadhi, and panna.

I would probably phrase things differently than @vividvoid, but the thrust is correct, IMO.

Also, a lot of maturation and development I've undergone in residential monastic training as a function of constant communal living and close teacher-student relationship is not always accessible, so while not a replacement, I'm hopeful offerings like what Gabe has planned will be helpful to people.

text:
My teacher is coming out of a long retreat in a few weeks and offering this public teaching on the 26th.
Straight from the heart, spreading the word, registration linked below.

"Join Lama Justin for this day-long workshop on Rangjung Dorje's celebrated prayer known as the Aspiration of Mahāmudrā. This luminous text is one of the most beloved and essential expressions of the Mahāmudrā view in the Kagyü tradition, a profound aspiration, teaching, and direct pointing-out of the nature of mind. Whether you are new to Mahāmudrā or have an existing practice, this workshop offers an opportunity to go deeper into the meaning and experience of these timeless verses.

Rangjung Dorje, the Third Karmapa, was one of the most brilliant and realized masters of the Tibetan tradition. His writings synthesized Mahāmudrā and Dzogchen in ways that continue to reverberate through Vajrayana today. The Aspiration of Mahāmudrā brings to life in an essentialized manner the essence of the path, allowing one to explore spontaneous liberation of appearance through these verses of stunning clarity and depth.

In times like these, when our world feels increasingly unstable and the ground beneath our feet uncertain, aspiration practices like this are vitally important. They are a way of orienting the mind toward what is most true, most stable, and most awake and of dedicating that recognition toward the benefit of all beings. Lama Justin will guide participants through the text with teachings, practice, and group exploration, bringing these instructions alive in a supportive and dynamic container.

This event is also a fundraiser in support of Yangti's ongoing development of while working to keep teachings accessible and sustainable. We are grateful for your generosity and practice.

The day-long workshop will be recorded for participants who are unable to attend live.

Event Schedule:

Sunday April 26th 10:00 am - 12:00 pm est
Introduction, Teachings & Practice
2:00 pm - 4:00 pm est
Practice, Concluding Teachings, & Group Sharing

"According to the wise, all is neither true nor false,
Like the moon’s reflection in water.
The ordinary mind itself is the dharmadhātu,
Which is called "essence of the victorious."

Rangjung Dorje

text:
"By way of further explanation of the nature of this released mind, we may drop a hint through the analogy of the film and the drama, which we have employed throughout. Now, for instance, in order to produce a tragic scene on the screen, the film producers adopt subtle devices and camera tricks. Sometimes an awe-inspiring scene of conflagration or ruthless arson, which drives terror into the hearts of the audience, is produced with the help of cardboard houses. Cardboard houses are set on fire, but the audience is hoodwinked into thinking that a huge mansion is on fire. Similarly, terrific traffic accidents are displayed on the screen with the help of a few toys. 

In this drama of existence, too, there are similar tragic scenes. Now, in spite of their tragic quality, if any member of the audience truly understands at that moment that these are cardboard houses and toys toppled from hill tops, he sees something comic in the apparently tragic. Likewise, in this drama of existence, there is a tragic aspect as well as a comic aspect.

As a matter of fact, both these words, tragic and comic, can be accommodated within the highly significant term samvega, anguish, sense of urgency. In trying to arouse samvega with regard to sankhāras, or preparations, we could bring in both these attitudes."

- Nibbana – The Mind Stilled,
Ven. Katukurunde Nanananda Thera

text:
"It’s hard to see the unaffected,
for the truth is not easily seen.
Craving is pierced
in one who knows;
for one who sees,
there is nothing."

Ud 8:2, Nibbana

"One who is dependent has wavering. One who is independent has no wavering. There being no wavering, there is calm. There being calm, there is no yearning. There being no yearning, there is no coming or going. There being no coming or going, there is no passing away or arising. There being no passing away or arising, there is neither a here nor a there nor a between-the-two. This, just this, is the end of stress."

Ud 8:4, Nibbana

text:
Developing stable attention is a skill to develop that takes time and repetition like any other, and many people's pre-existent habits around performance, identity, shame, anything and everything will surface in the process to be seen and released. That's a large part of the benefit!
The fact of initial unintentional, persistent, and recurrent mind-wandering is an occasion for insight and self-compassion if properly introduced, rather than the issues you mention.
Alas, unfortunately poor pedagogy is common, and general misunderstandings are the norm for those beginning, often without any personal instruction.

text:
Even when you're right, you're still wrong.

“There is the case, monks, where a certain contemplative or brahman, with the relinquishing of speculations about the past and the relinquishing of speculations about the future, from being totally not determined on the fetters of sensuality, surmounting the rapture of seclusion, surmounting pleasure not-of-the-flesh, and surmounting the feeling of neither pleasure nor pain, envisions that ‘I am at peace, I am unbound, I am without clinging/sustenance!’

“With regard to this, the Tathāgata discerns that ‘This venerable contemplative or brahman, with the relinquishing of speculations about the past and the relinquishing of speculations about the future, from being totally not determined on the fetters of sensuality, surmounting the rapture of seclusion, surmounting pleasure not-of-the-flesh, and surmounting the feeling of neither pleasure nor pain, envisions that “I am at peace, I am unbound, I am without clinging/sustenance!” Yes, he affirms a practice conducive to unbinding. But still he clings, clinging to a speculation about the past; or he clings, clinging to a speculation about the future; or he clings, clinging to a fetter of sensuality; or he clings, clinging to the rapture of seclusion; or he clings, clinging to pleasure not-of-the-flesh; or he clings, clinging to a feeling of neither pleasure nor pain. And the fact that he envisions that “I am at peace, I am unbound, I am without clinging/sustenance!”—that in itself points to his clinging. With regard to that—fabricated, gross—there is still the cessation of fabrications: There is this.’ Knowing that, seeing the escape from it, the Tathāgata has gone beyond it."

text:
Noticing, not for the first time, that all the same squicky social dynamics which contributed to my departure from the commune years back are present, if in a differently mediated fashion, and with more public consequence, in TPOT.

The same debates, the same policing of association, the same jockeying over implicit and explicit social power, the same rounds of controversy over polarizing community members.

I'm sensitive to it, still, I find. My ex and some of my close friends were very badly hurt by community furor, some of which I later learned was consciously dishonest and Machiavellian, some just confused and inflamed by reactive emotion and factional allegiances.

I behaved quite cowardly through it all, I admit. Afraid to take a stand and get schwacked with collateral shrapnel.

What's true? What's exaggeration? What's the motivation? What's the best course of action? Easy, so easy, to get it all wrong.

You get a densely connected network of people socializing, working, and dating amongst each other, shit happens.

My heart hurts.
