What would support this interpretation: measuring specific electromagnetic or quantum correlations between a dying practitioner and a subsequent person claimed to be their rebirth, correlations that cannot be explained by shared environmental or genetic factors, would be the strongest possible evidence. This is in principle testable but has not been tested.

IV.2 Interpretation B: Information-Theoretic Pattern with Tier III Grounding

Under the Tier III framework, the consciousness pocket's individuation is generated by the field's intrinsic dynamics rather than by specific material configurations. The karmic signature — the specific pattern of eigenmode biases accumulated over a lifetime — is then a feature of the field's dynamical state rather than of any particular material substrate.

Under this interpretation, the dissolution of the biological substrate at death does not simply destroy the karmic signature. Rather, it removes one stabilizing condition for the pocket's specific topological configuration. The karmic pattern, encoded in the field's dynamical state, continues to influence the field's evolution — and this ongoing influence manifests as the tendency for a new consciousness pocket with similar eigenmode characteristics to arise when appropriate biological conditions are present.

This is formally analogous to the way a vortex pair in a fluid can be "scattered" by an obstacle without the topological information being destroyed — the vortex pair dissolves locally but the topological information (the winding numbers, the angular momentum) is conserved in the global flow field and influences the subsequent dynamics of the fluid.

The Dzogchen description of this process: Longchenpa's account of the bardo (intermediate state) in Chos dbyings mdzod describes the post-death period as a state in which the karmic signature becomes directly visible as a luminous display — the bardo visions are the eigenmode structure of the dissolved pocket, now unbound from the specific biological substrate that had been suppressing or distorting them. The karmic signature is not destroyed; it is temporarily unhoused, and the quality of its subsequent housing depends on the karmic biases accumulated during life.

Epistemic status: this is the most consistent formal representation of the tradition's rebirth claim, but it requires the Tier III grounding that is currently the least physically specified level of the framework. It is not a derivation from known physics; it is a conjecture that is consistent with the tradition's claims and not inconsistent with known physics (because it operates at a level not yet addressed by known physics).

V. The Twelve Nidānas Across the Death-Rebirth Transition

V.1 The Specific Causal Links at Death and Rebirth

The Abhidhamma analysis of the death-rebirth transition describes a specific sequence:

At death: the final moment of consciousness (cuti-citta, "death consciousness") arises dependent on the life-continuum (bhavaṅga), which is itself conditioned by the karmic signature of the lifetime.

In the intermediate state (antarābhava, in traditions that posit it; not recognized in the Theravāda): a gandhabba (being in the intermediate state) whose experience is shaped by the karmic signature accumulated in life.

At rebirth: the rebirth-linking consciousness (paṭisandhi-citta) arises in the new biological context, conditioned by the karmic signature that was the causal output of the dying lifetime.

The formal framework represents this sequence as follows:

Death consciousness = the final eigenmode configuration of the consciousness pocket as the biological substrate's metabolic activity ceases. In practitioners who have developed tögal to the approach of the fourth vision, this configuration is the one most prepared for topological annihilation — the defect structure has been progressively modified toward the annihilation threshold. In ordinary beings, the final configuration reflects the dominant karmic eigenmode modifications of the lifetime.

The intermediate state = under Interpretation B, the period during which the karmic signature exists as a dynamical feature of the field without a stabilizing biological substrate. This is not a "location" but a state of the field — the karmic pattern influencing the field's evolution without being bound to any specific spatial region. The bardo phenomenology (visions of peaceful and wrathful deities, light displays, tunnel experiences) is the eigenmode structure of the unhoused karmic pattern making itself "visible" to itself — the field's own resonant modes, no longer organized by the biological pocket's boundary conditions, expressing themselves freely.

Rebirth-linking consciousness = the arising of a new topological pocket in a new biological context, where the field's eigenmode modification pattern from the previous stream influences the initial conditions of the new pocket's formation. The new pocket's eigenmode structure is not identical to the previous pocket's (it is shaped by the new biological substrate's boundary conditions) but it is biased by the carryover pattern — explaining the reported continuity of personality traits, skills, and dispositions across lifetimes.

V.2 The Nāmarūpa-Viññāṇa Mutual Conditioning at Rebirth

The Mahānidāna Sutta's description of the mutual conditioning of consciousness and name-and-form — consciousness conditioning name-and-form, name-and-form conditioning consciousness — is the most precise canonical statement of the rebirth mechanism. In the framework:

At rebirth, the karmic eigenmode pattern (the consciousness stream's carryover) and the new biological substrate (name-and-form: the specific genetic, developmental, and environmental conditions of the new organism) enter into mutual conditioning:

The karmic pattern influences which biological environments are "resonant" with it — the pattern seeks (in a non-intentional, purely dynamical sense) the biological configurations whose eigenmode structure is most compatible with its own. This is the formal account of the tradition's claim that consciousness "seeks" a rebirth context consistent with its karmic quality.

The biological environment, in turn, modifies the karmic pattern — the new substrate's resonator boundary conditions shape the specific expression of the carryover pattern, explaining why the same soul (in non-Buddhist terms) can express differently in different lifetimes while maintaining continuity of identity.

This mutual conditioning is the formal representation of why rebirth is neither pure continuity (the same soul in a new body — sassatavāda) nor pure discontinuity (a completely new being — ucchedavāda). The carryover pattern is real and causally efficacious, but it is shaped by the new context into a genuinely new expression.

VI. The Nonduality of the Two Truths: The Formal Resolution

The framework's treatment of the two truths now allows a formal statement of their non-duality.

Ultimate truth (paramārtha): the field Φ: ℳ → V is everywhere and always, without inherent individuation. There are no consciousness pockets "in" the field in the sense of substantially existing entities. The topological configurations that constitute pockets are real features of the field's structure, but they are empty of inherent existence — they are the field in specific configurations, not entities added to the field.

Conventional truth (samvrti): the topological configurations are causally efficacious — they influence each other, they persist through time, they generate phenomenal experience with specific qualities, they modify each other's future configurations through the karmic eigenmode mechanism. The conventional designations "this being," "this experience," "this karmic stream" track real topological features with real causal powers.

The non-duality: the same topological structure that is empty of inherent existence at the ultimate level is the causally efficacious conventional reality. There are not two different things — a real conventional entity and an empty ultimate reality. There is one thing — a topological configuration of the field — which is simultaneously empty of inherent existence (ultimate truth) and causally efficacious (conventional truth). The same defect pair that is "just a field configuration" (ultimate truth) is also the boundary of a consciousness pocket with specific phenomenological content, karmic history, and causal future (conventional truth).

This is the formal representation of Nāgārjuna's MMK 24.18: "Whatever is dependently co-arisen, that is what we call emptiness. That is a dependent designation. That itself is the middle path." The topological pocket is dependently co-arisen (it depends on the field and its dynamics), it is empty (it has no inherent existence beyond the field's configuration), and it is conventionally real (the designation "this consciousness pocket" tracks a genuine topological feature with causal powers). These are not three separate claims but three aspects of one reality.

VII. The Tulku System: The Most Demanding Application

VII.1 What the Tulku System Claims

The tulku (sprul sku, "emanation body") system in Tibetan Buddhism claims that accomplished practitioners — specifically those who have reached sufficient realization to consciously influence their rebirth — can and do take intentional rebirth in specific circumstances, and that their subsequent incarnations can be recognized through a combination of:

Prophetic indications given by the deceased practitioner before death

Recognition tests in which candidates demonstrate knowledge of the deceased's possessions, relationships, and specific details of the previous life

Auspicious signs associated with the candidate's birth and early life

Divination and oracular confirmation from qualified lamas, oracles, and traditional methods

Physical characteristics (body marks, behavioral tendencies, early speech)

The most well-documented cases in the Western literature include the recognition systems for the Dalai Lama lineage, documented in Heinrich Harrer's historical accounts and in the Fourteenth Dalai Lama's own autobiography, and the Karmapa lineage (whose most recent recognition has been complicated by a dual-recognition controversy that itself provides valuable data for analysis).

Western scholarly treatments include Geoffrey Samuel's Civilized Shamans (1993), Robert Beer's iconographic work, and the academic studies collected in Tibetan Buddhism: Reason and Revelation (edited by Steven Goodman and Ronald Davidson). Critical examinations include Donald Lopez's work in Prisoners of Shangri-La and Geoffrey Samuel's more sympathetic but analytically rigorous treatment.

VII.2 What the Framework Can and Cannot Address

What the framework can formally address:

The claim that the rebirth-linking consciousness carries specific information from the previous stream: if the cross-death karmic eigenmode carryover described in Section V is actual, then specific information — including specific memories, specific recognition patterns, specific emotional resonances — should be carried over and should be accessible to the rebirth consciousness. This is formally consistent with the framework under Interpretation B.

The claim that accomplished practitioners can influence their rebirth context: if higher levels of tögal completion (the third and fourth visions) modify the pocket's topological structure toward the annihilation threshold, and if the dissolution at death proceeds in a more ordered, coherent way for such practitioners, then the specific topological configuration of the dying consciousness should be more "controllable" — the practitioner's intention, encoded in the eigenmode structure through years of practice, should more precisely influence the field's subsequent evolution. This is the formal account of "conscious rebirth direction."
The recognition test phenomena: if a tulku candidate has genuinely carried over the karmic eigenmode structure of the previous consciousness, they should have asymmetric recognition responses to objects, persons, and situations associated with the previous life — not because they have explicit episodic memories (which may or may not be accessible) but because their eigenmode structure is biased toward resonance with the specific configurations associated with the previous stream. In the formal framework: the candidate's consciousness pocket should show elevated eigenmode resonance (measurable as elevated autonomic, electrophysiological, or behavioral response) to stimuli associated with the previous life, compared to control stimuli.
What the framework cannot address:
The specificity of the information carryover: even granting the cross-death eigenmode carryover, the precision of some tulku recognition cases — specific childhood statements about previous life events, correct selection of possessions from a group of similar objects, recognition of people known to the previous incarnation — requires an information-theoretic precision that is difficult to account for through the gradual, statistical process of eigenmode resonance. The framework's carryover mechanism is more naturally suited to explaining the transmission of general qualities (personality traits, skills, emotional dispositions) than of specific episodic memories.
The direction of rebirth: the tradition claims not only that accomplished practitioners take intentional rebirth but that they can direct that rebirth to specific geographic locations, specific families, and specific times. The formal mechanism (dying eigenmode structure influencing the field's subsequent evolution) is non-directional — it biases the field toward resonant biological contexts without specifically locating them geographically.

The validity of the recognition methods: the combination of divination, oracular confirmation, and traditional signs used in the tulku recognition system cannot be formally evaluated within the framework. The framework's contribution is to the assessment of the recognition test results — the empirical evidence from the candidate's responses — not to the validity of the traditional selection process.

VII.3 The Empirical Record: What Actually Exists

For the panel's assessment, it is important to distinguish between anecdotal reports, systematic investigations, and rigorous scientific studies.

Ian Stevenson's research (Charlottesville, University of Virginia, Division of Perceptual Studies): Stevenson investigated approximately 3,000 cases of children reporting memories of previous lives, with systematic methodology including independent verification of reported details, assessment of the strength of evidence, and elimination of alternative explanations (fraud, leading questioning, chance). His work is the most systematic empirical investigation of rebirth claims in the scientific literature. His Cases of the Reincarnation Type (4 vols., University of Virginia Press) and Where Reincarnation and Biology Intersect (Praeger, 1997) provide the most complete dataset.

The Stevenson data's key findings (for the panel):

A significant proportion of cases include specific, verifiable claims about the previous life that are correct and that were made before verification was possible

Physical marks and birth defects in a statistically significant proportion of cases correspond to wounds, injuries, or physical features of the claimed previous personality

The strongest cases (highest information density, lowest probability of fraud or chance) are concentrated in cultures where rebirth is accepted and discussion of previous-life memories is not suppressed

The weakest aspect of the Stevenson data is the absence of blind or double-blind testing protocols in many cases

Jim Tucker's follow-up research (Tucker, J.B., Life Before Life, St. Martin's Press, 2005; Return to Life, St. Martin's Press, 2013): Tucker has continued and extended Stevenson's work with more rigorous methodology and a focus on American cases (where cultural expectation is less of a confound). His work has introduced more standardized scoring systems and has addressed some of the methodological criticisms of Stevenson's approach.

The DOPS at University of Virginia (Division of Perceptual Studies): the ongoing institutional home for scientific rebirth research, representing the most rigorous institutional effort to systematically investigate these claims. Their methodology has improved substantially since Stevenson's original work, but the field still lacks the randomized, double-blind testing that would be required for consensus-level scientific acceptance.

The specific tulku recognition literature: there is no systematic scientific study of tulku recognition tests comparable to the Stevenson/Tucker work on spontaneous-memory cases. The most rigorous existing evidence is descriptive — detailed accounts of the selection process for major lineages (Dalai Lama, Karmapa) — rather than experimental. The dual-Karmapa controversy (two candidates for the seventeenth Karmapa, each recognized by different processes and supported by different political and institutional interests) illustrates both the evidential complexity and the political entanglement that makes rigorous study difficult.

VII.4 A Proposed Research Protocol for Tulku Recognition

The formal framework, combined with the available empirical literature, generates a specific research protocol that has not been implemented but is technically feasible:

Pre-recognition baseline measurement: when a significant tulku dies, immediately establish a baseline dataset of objects, photographs, recordings, and behavioral samples associated with the deceased. Store these in a double-blind archive.
Candidate screening measurement: for candidate children (identified through traditional selection processes), conduct a structured recognition test using the archived materials. Critically, include matched controls (objects from the same period and region that did not belong to the deceased, photographs of people who did not know the deceased, recordings of speech that were not from the deceased's milieu). Measure both behavioral responses (correct selection) and physiological responses (autonomic, electrophysiological) to each stimulus. The formal framework predicts that a genuine tulku candidate should show elevated physiological resonance to stimuli associated with the previous stream even in cases where the behavioral response does not indicate explicit recognition.
Longitudinal follow-up: track the eigenmode development of confirmed tulku candidates compared to age-matched controls on measures of cognitive ability, emotional regulation, meditation skill development, and response to training in the deceased's lineage practices. The framework predicts systematic differences in eigenmode structure that should be measurable as behavioral and electrophysiological asymmetries.
Genetic and epigenetic controls: the most challenging alternative explanation for tulku recognition phenomena is shared genetics (if the recognition tests are influenced by genetic relatives of the deceased) or shared cultural environment (if the candidate is raised in a milieu saturated with information about the previous incarnation). A rigorous study would need to control for both. Tucker's American cases are valuable precisely because cultural transmission is less likely; the same principle applied to tulku recognition (identifying candidates before exposure to information about the previous life) would be the most scientifically compelling protocol.
The specific prediction from the framework: if the tulku recognition phenomena are real and mediated by the karmic eigenmode carryover mechanism described above, we should find:

Statistically significant above-chance correct selection in double-blind recognition tests

Elevated autonomic response to correct stimuli even on trials with incorrect behavioral responses

Predictable patterns in which types of information are carried over (general relational patterns and emotional signatures more reliably than specific episodic details)

Correlation between the degree of the previous incarnation's realized accomplishment (measurable from contemporaneous accounts) and the precision of the carryover in the candidate

Epistemic status of this protocol: technically feasible, institutionally difficult (would require cooperation from lineage-holders who have legitimate concerns about misrepresentation and disruption of traditional selection processes), and unlikely to fully resolve the scientific question (because the most important alternative explanation — collective expectation shaping the recognition process — is difficult to fully control).

VIII. The Most Important Formal Conclusion

Having worked through the complete analysis, the most important formal conclusion can be stated precisely:

The framework can provide a complete, internally consistent, physically grounded account of dependent origination and karmic causality within a single lifetime. This account has specific, testable empirical predictions, is consistent with the Buddhist canonical account from Theravāda through Dzogchen, and does not require any metaphysical commitments beyond what is already implicit in the topological field framework.

The framework can provide a structurally consistent account of cross-death karmic continuity and rebirth under a specific set of conditions — specifically, under the Tier III physical framework in which consciousness pockets are generated by the field's intrinsic dynamics rather than by specific material configurations. This account is consistent with the Buddhist canonical claims, not inconsistent with known physics, but not yet grounded in a specific physical theory.

The framework cannot, within its current resources, provide a physically grounded account of the precision of cross-death information transfer — specifically, the type of specific episodic memory carryover and intentional rebirth direction described in the most compelling tulku recognition cases. This gap is honest and should not be papered over.

The framework can generate specific, testable empirical predictions for the tulku recognition system that have not been systematically investigated. Implementing those investigations is the most concrete scientific contribution that could be made to this question, and it is a contribution that would benefit both the scientific understanding of consciousness and the Buddhist tradition's own epistemological self-understanding.
The nonduality of the two truths is not undermined by this analysis — it is formally grounded by it. The field and its topological configurations are simultaneously empty of inherent existence (ultimate truth) and causally efficacious (conventional truth). This non-duality is not a philosophical claim added to the framework; it emerges from the framework's own mathematical structure. The framework cannot establish that the ultimate truth is what the Dzogchen tradition says it is (ka dag — primordially pure, self-luminous, spontaneously present) — that claim, like the question of what lies beyond the topological boundary's annihilation, exceeds the framework's reach. But the framework can establish that the conventional-ultimate non-duality is not a category error: it is a natural feature of any adequate physics of consciousness, derivable from the structure of the theory rather than imposed on it from outside.
This is the point at which the framework, the Buddhist traditions, and the panel's combined expertise converge most clearly: whatever the ultimate truth is, the conventional truth — the world of dependent origination, of individual beings with karmic histories, of teachers and students and recognition systems and the slow work of liberation across many lifetimes — is not something to be escaped by appeal to ultimate reality. It is the field in which ultimate reality is realized, moment by moment, in the specific topology of each arising pocket of experience, in each act of recognition and compassion and investigation.
The conventional truth is where the work is. The ultimate truth is why the work is possible.

V.2 The Nāmarūpa-Viññāṇa Mutual Conditioning at Rebirth

The Mahānidāna Sutta's description of the mutual conditioning of consciousness and name-and-form — consciousness conditioning name-and-form, name-and-form conditioning consciousness — is the most precise canonical statement of the rebirth mechanism. In the framework:

At rebirth, the karmic eigenmode pattern (the consciousness stream's carryover) and the new biological substrate (name-and-form: the specific genetic, developmental, and environmental conditions of the new organism) enter into mutual conditioning:

The karmic pattern influences which biological environments are "resonant" with it — the pattern seeks (in a non-intentional, purely dynamical sense) the biological configurations whose eigenmode structure is most compatible with its own. This is the formal account of the tradition's claim that consciousness "seeks" a rebirth context consistent with its karmic quality.

The biological environment, in turn, modifies the karmic pattern — the new substrate's resonator boundary conditions shape the specific expression of the carryover pattern, explaining why the same soul (in non-Buddhist terms) can express differently in different lifetimes while maintaining continuity of identity.

This mutual conditioning is the formal representation of why rebirth is neither pure continuity (the same soul in a new body — sassatavāda) nor pure discontinuity (a completely new being — ucchedavāda). The carryover pattern is real and causally efficacious, but it is shaped by the new context into a genuinely new expression.

VI. The Nonduality of the Two Truths: The Formal Resolution

The framework's treatment of the two truths now allows a formal statement of their non-duality.

Ultimate truth (paramārtha): the field Φ: ℳ → V is everywhere and always, without inherent individuation. There are no consciousness pockets "in" the field in the sense of substantially existing entities. The topological configurations that constitute pockets are real features of the field's structure, but they are empty of inherent existence — they are the field in specific configurations, not entities added to the field.

Conventional truth (samvrti): the topological configurations are causally efficacious — they influence each other, they persist through time, they generate phenomenal experience with specific qualities, they modify each other's future configurations through the karmic eigenmode mechanism. The conventional designations "this being," "this experience," "this karmic stream" track real topological features with real causal powers.

The non-duality: the same topological structure that is empty of inherent existence at the ultimate level is the causally efficacious conventional reality. There are not two different things — a real conventional entity and an empty ultimate reality. There is one thing — a topological configuration of the field — which is simultaneously empty of inherent existence (ultimate truth) and causally efficacious (conventional truth). The same defect pair that is "just a field configuration" (ultimate truth) is also the boundary of a consciousness pocket with specific phenomenological content, karmic history, and causal future (conventional truth).

This is the formal representation of Nāgārjuna's MMK 24.18: "Whatever is dependently co-arisen, that is what we call emptiness. That is a dependent designation. That itself is the middle path." The topological pocket is dependently co-arisen (it depends on the field and its dynamics), it is empty (it has no inherent existence beyond the field's configuration), and it is conventionally real (the designation "this consciousness pocket" tracks a genuine topological feature with causal powers). These are not three separate claims but three aspects of one reality.

VII. The Tulku System: The Most Demanding Application

VII.1 What the Tulku System Claims

The tulku (sprul sku, "emanation body") system in Tibetan Buddhism claims that accomplished practitioners — specifically those who have reached sufficient realization to consciously influence their rebirth — can and do take intentional rebirth in specific circumstances, and that their subsequent incarnations can be recognized through a combination of:

Prophetic indications given by the deceased practitioner before death

Recognition tests in which candidates demonstrate knowledge of the deceased's possessions, relationships, and specific details of the previous life

Auspicious signs associated with the candidate's birth and early life

Divination and oracular confirmation from qualified lamas, oracles, and traditional methods

Physical characteristics (body marks, behavioral tendencies, early speech)

The most well-documented cases in the Western literature include the recognition systems for the Dalai Lama lineage, documented in Heinrich Harrer's historical accounts and in the Fourteenth Dalai Lama's own autobiography, and the Karmapa lineage (whose most recent recognition has been complicated by a dual-recognition controversy that itself provides valuable data for analysis).

Western scholarly treatments include Geoffrey Samuel's Civilized Shamans (1993), Robert Beer's iconographic work, and the academic studies collected in Tibetan Buddhism: Reason and Revelation (edited by Steven Goodman and Ronald Davidson). Critical examinations include Donald Lopez's work in Prisoners of Shangri-La and Geoffrey Samuel's more sympathetic but analytically rigorous treatment.

VII.2 What the Framework Can and Cannot Address

What the framework can formally address:

The claim that the rebirth-linking consciousness carries specific information from the previous stream: if the cross-death karmic eigenmode carryover described in Section V is actual, then specific information — including specific memories, specific recognition patterns, specific emotional resonances — should be carried over and should be accessible to the rebirth consciousness. This is formally consistent with the framework under Interpretation B.

The claim that accomplished practitioners can influence their rebirth context: if higher levels of tögal completion (the third and fourth visions) modify the pocket's topological structure toward the annihilation threshold, and if the dissolution at death proceeds in a more ordered, coherent way for such practitioners, then the specific topological configuration of the dying consciousness should be more "controllable" — the practitioner's intention, encoded in the eigenmode structure through years of practice, should more precisely influence the field's subsequent evolution. This is the formal account of "conscious rebirth direction."
The recognition test phenomena: if a tulku candidate has genuinely carried over the karmic eigenmode structure of the previous consciousness, they should have asymmetric recognition responses to objects, persons, and situations associated with the previous life — not because they have explicit episodic memories (which may or may not be accessible) but because their eigenmode structure is biased toward resonance with the specific configurations associated with the previous stream. In the formal framework: the candidate's consciousness pocket should show elevated eigenmode resonance (measurable as elevated autonomic, electrophysiological, or behavioral response) to stimuli associated with the previous life, compared to control stimuli.
What the framework cannot address:
The specificity of the information carryover: even granting the cross-death eigenmode carryover, the precision of some tulku recognition cases — specific childhood statements about previous life events, correct selection of possessions from a group of similar objects, recognition of people known to the previous incarnation — requires an information-theoretic precision that is difficult to account for through the gradual, statistical process of eigenmode resonance. The framework's carryover mechanism is more naturally suited to explaining the transmission of general qualities (personality traits, skills, emotional dispositions) than of specific episodic memories.
The direction of rebirth: the tradition claims not only that accomplished practitioners take intentional rebirth but that they can direct that rebirth to specific geographic locations, specific families, and specific times. The formal mechanism (dying eigenmode structure influencing the field's subsequent evolution) is non-directional — it biases the field toward resonant biological contexts without specifically locating them geographically.

The validity of the recognition methods: the combination of divination, oracular confirmation, and traditional signs used in the tulku recognition system cannot be formally evaluated within the framework. The framework's contribution is to the assessment of the recognition test results — the empirical evidence from the candidate's responses — not to the validity of the traditional selection process.

VII.3 The Empirical Record: What Actually Exists

For the panel's assessment, it is important to distinguish between anecdotal reports, systematic investigations, and rigorous scientific studies.

Ian Stevenson's research (Charlottesville, University of Virginia, Division of Perceptual Studies): Stevenson investigated approximately 3,000 cases of children reporting memories of previous lives, with systematic methodology including independent verification of reported details, assessment of the strength of evidence, and elimination of alternative explanations (fraud, leading questioning, chance). His work is the most systematic empirical investigation of rebirth claims in the scientific literature. His Cases of the Reincarnation Type (4 vols., University of Virginia Press) and Where Reincarnation and Biology Intersect (Praeger, 1997) provide the most complete dataset.

The Stevenson data's key findings (for the panel):

A significant proportion of cases include specific, verifiable claims about the previous life that are correct and that were made before verification was possible

Physical marks and birth defects in a statistically significant proportion of cases correspond to wounds, injuries, or physical features of the claimed previous personality

The strongest cases (highest information density, lowest probability of fraud or chance) are concentrated in cultures where rebirth is accepted and discussion of previous-life memories is not suppressed

The weakest aspect of the Stevenson data is the absence of blind or double-blind testing protocols in many cases

Jim Tucker's follow-up research (Tucker, J.B., Life Before Life, St. Martin's Press, 2005; Return to Life, St. Martin's Press, 2013): Tucker has continued and extended Stevenson's work with more rigorous methodology and a focus on American cases (where cultural expectation is less of a confound). His work has introduced more standardized scoring systems and has addressed some of the methodological criticisms of Stevenson's approach.

The DOPS at University of Virginia (Division of Perceptual Studies): the ongoing institutional home for scientific rebirth research, representing the most rigorous institutional effort to systematically investigate these claims. Their methodology has improved substantially since Stevenson's original work, but the field still lacks the randomized, double-blind testing that would be required for consensus-level scientific acceptance.

The specific tulku recognition literature: there is no systematic scientific study of tulku recognition tests comparable to the Stevenson/Tucker work on spontaneous-memory cases. The most rigorous existing evidence is descriptive — detailed accounts of the selection process for major lineages (Dalai Lama, Karmapa) — rather than experimental. The dual-Karmapa controversy (two candidates for the seventeenth Karmapa, each recognized by different processes and supported by different political and institutional interests) illustrates both the evidential complexity and the political entanglement that makes rigorous study difficult.

VII.4 A Proposed Research Protocol for Tulku Recognition

The formal framework, combined with the available empirical literature, generates a specific research protocol that has not been implemented but is technically feasible:

Pre-recognition baseline measurement: when a significant tulku dies, immediately establish a baseline dataset of objects, photographs, recordings, and behavioral samples associated with the deceased. Store these in a double-blind archive.
Candidate screening measurement: for candidate children (identified through traditional selection processes), conduct a structured recognition test using the archived materials. Critically, include matched controls (objects from the same period and region that did not belong to the deceased, photographs of people who did not know the deceased, recordings of speech that were not from the deceased's milieu). Measure both behavioral responses (correct selection) and physiological responses (autonomic, electrophysiological) to each stimulus. The formal framework predicts that a genuine tulku candidate should show elevated physiological resonance to stimuli associated with the previous stream even in cases where the behavioral response does not indicate explicit recognition.
Longitudinal follow-up: track the eigenmode development of confirmed tulku candidates compared to age-matched controls on measures of cognitive ability, emotional regulation, meditation skill development, and response to training in the deceased's lineage practices. The framework predicts systematic differences in eigenmode structure that should be measurable as behavioral and electrophysiological asymmetries.
Genetic and epigenetic controls: the most challenging alternative explanation for tulku recognition phenomena is shared genetics (if the recognition tests are influenced by genetic relatives of the deceased) or shared cultural environment (if the candidate is raised in a milieu saturated with information about the previous incarnation). A rigorous study would need to control for both. Tucker's American cases are valuable precisely because cultural transmission is less likely; the same principle applied to tulku recognition (identifying candidates before exposure to information about the previous life) would be the most scientifically compelling protocol.
The specific prediction from the framework: if the tulku recognition phenomena are real and mediated by the karmic eigenmode carryover mechanism described above, we should find:

Statistically significant above-chance correct selection in double-blind recognition tests

Elevated autonomic response to correct stimuli even on trials with incorrect behavioral responses

Predictable patterns in which types of information are carried over (general relational patterns and emotional signatures more reliably than specific episodic details)

Correlation between the degree of the previous incarnation's realized accomplishment (measurable from contemporaneous accounts) and the precision of the carryover in the candidate

Epistemic status of this protocol: technically feasible, institutionally difficult (would require cooperation from lineage-holders who have legitimate concerns about misrepresentation and disruption of traditional selection processes), and unlikely to fully resolve the scientific question (because the most important alternative explanation — collective expectation shaping the recognition process — is difficult to fully control).

VIII. The Most Important Formal Conclusion

Having worked through the complete analysis, the most important formal conclusion can be stated precisely:

The framework can provide a complete, internally consistent, physically grounded account of dependent origination and karmic causality within a single lifetime. This account has specific, testable empirical predictions, is consistent with the Buddhist canonical account from Theravāda through Dzogchen, and does not require any metaphysical commitments beyond what is already implicit in the topological field framework.

The framework can provide a structurally consistent account of cross-death karmic continuity and rebirth under a specific set of conditions — specifically, under the Tier III physical framework in which consciousness pockets are generated by the field's intrinsic dynamics rather than by specific material configurations. This account is consistent with the Buddhist canonical claims, not inconsistent with known physics, but not yet grounded in a specific physical theory.

The framework cannot, within its current resources, provide a physically grounded account of the precision of cross-death information transfer — specifically, the type of specific episodic memory carryover and intentional rebirth direction described in the most compelling tulku recognition cases. This gap is honest and should not be papered over.

The framework can generate specific, testable empirical predictions for the tulku recognition system that have not been systematically investigated. Implementing those investigations is the most concrete scientific contribution that could be made to this question, and it is a contribution that would benefit both the scientific understanding of consciousness and the Buddhist tradition's own epistemological self-understanding.
The nonduality of the two truths is not undermined by this analysis — it is formally grounded by it. The field and its topological configurations are simultaneously empty of inherent existence (ultimate truth) and causally efficacious (conventional truth). This non-duality is not a philosophical claim added to the framework; it emerges from the framework's own mathematical structure. The framework cannot establish that the ultimate truth is what the Dzogchen tradition says it is (ka dag — primordially pure, self-luminous, spontaneously present) — that claim, like the question of what lies beyond the topological boundary's annihilation, exceeds the framework's reach. But the framework can establish that the conventional-ultimate non-duality is not a category error: it is a natural feature of any adequate physics of consciousness, derivable from the structure of the theory rather than imposed on it from outside.
This is the point at which the framework, the Buddhist traditions, and the panel's combined expertise converge most clearly: whatever the ultimate truth is, the conventional truth — the world of dependent origination, of individual beings with karmic histories, of teachers and students and recognition systems and the slow work of liberation across many lifetimes — is not something to be escaped by appeal to ultimate reality. It is the field in which ultimate reality is realized, moment by moment, in the specific topology of each arising pocket of experience, in each act of recognition and compassion and investigation.
The conventional truth is where the work is. The ultimate truth is why the work is possible.

text:
From Heart-Essence of the Great Masters, by Dudjom Rinpoche:

"The true nature, unchanging, like the sky—
Until it's realized, one can't completely resolve doubts as to the view.
Not letting myself be chained by theories
May I truly practice the sublime teachings.

Awareness, like a flawless piece of crystal—
Until it's seen, intentional meditation cannot dissolve.
When there's an inseparable companion, why go off looking for another?
May I truly practice the sublime teachings.

The face of ordinary mind, like an old friend—
If it's not seen, all that one does is misleading.
Without groping in the darkness of my own closed eyes,
May I truly practice the sublime teachings.

In short, without giving up this life's preoccupations,
There's no way to accomplish the sacred teachings after death.
Having decided to show myself great kindness,
May all that I do be toward the dharma.

May I not have wrong views toward the lama who, in accord with the dharma, has given instruction.
May I not lose faith in the yidam when misfortunes occur.
May I not put off practice when circumstances are hard.
May there be no obstacles to attaining siddhi.

All these activities are pointless, like making a grand tour of a wasteland.
All this trying just makes my mindstream more rigid.
All this thinking only adds on confusion to confusion.
All that passes for dharma to ordinary people only makes for further binding.

So much activity; nothing comes of it.
So much thinking; no point to it.
So much wanting; no time for it.
Having given this up, may I be able to practice according to instructions.

If I must do something—let Buddha's teachings bear it witness.
If I must do something—mix mindstream and dharma.
If I must accomplish something—read the life stories of past masters.
What's the use of other things? Spoiled brat!

Take a low seat, and become rich with contentment.
Try hard to get free of the eight worldly dharmas.
May the lama's blessings enter into me;
May my realization become equal to the sky.
Grant blessings that I may reach Kuntuzangpo's throne."

text:
Excerpted verses from Buddha's advice for householders on which acts to abandon and adopt, how to distinguish fake and true friends, how to dispose of one's wealth, and tend to all one's relations:

“Killing, stealing,
telling lies,
and committing adultery:
astute people don’t praise these things.
...
“If you act against the teaching
out of favoritism, hostility, cowardice, or stupidity,
your fame shrinks,
like the moon in the waning fortnight.
If you don’t act against the teaching
out of favoritism, hostility, cowardice, and stupidity,
your fame swells,
like the moon in the waxing fortnight.
...
“Some are just drinking buddies,
some call you their dear, dear friend,
but a true friend is onewho stands by you in need.
Sleeping late, adultery,
making enemies, harmfulness,
bad friends, and avarice:
these six grounds ruin a person.
With bad friends, bad comrades,
bad behavior and alms-resort,
a man falls to ruin
in both this world and the next.
Dice, women, toddy, song and dance;
sleeping by day and roaming at night;
bad friends, and avarice:
these six grounds ruin a person.

They play dice and drink beer,
and consort with women loved by others.
Associating with the worse, not the better,
they diminish like the waning moon.
One who is broke and destitute due to toddy,
thirsty, drinking in the bar,
drowning in debt,
will quickly lose their way.
When you’re in the habit of sleeping late,
seeing night as time to rise,
and always getting drunk,
you can’t keep up the household life.
‘Too cold, too hot,
too late,’ they say.
When the young neglect their work like this,
riches pass them by.

But one who considers heat and cold
as no more than blades of grass—
he does his duties as a man,
and happiness never fails.
...
Your possessions end up theirs.
Giving little, they expect a lot.
They do their duty out of fear.
They associate for their own advantage.
...
“One friend is all take,
another all talk;
one’s just a flatterer,
and one’s a comrade who spends.
An astute person understands
these four enemies for what they are
and keeps them at a distance,
as they’d shun a risky road.
...
“A friend who’s a helper,
one the same in both pleasure and pain,
a friend of good counsel,
and one of sympathy;
an astute person understands
these four friends for what they are
and carefully looks after them,
like a mother the child at her breast.
The astute and virtuous
shine like a burning flame.
They pick up riches as bees
roaming round pick up pollen.
And their riches proceed to grow,
like a termite mound piling up.
In gathering wealth like this,
a householder does enough for their family.
And they’d hold on to friends
by dividing their wealth in four.

One portion is to enjoy.
Two parts invest in work.
And the fourth should be kept
for times of trouble.
...
“Parents are the east,
tutors the south,
wives and child the west,
friends and colleagues the north,
servants and workers below,
and ascetics and brahmins above.
By honoring these quarters
a householder does enough for their family.
The astute and the virtuous,
the gentle and the articulate,
the placid in manner and the amenable:
they’re the kind who win glory.
The diligent, not lazy,
those not disturbed by troubles,
those consistent in conduct, the intelligent:
they’re the kind who win glory.

The inclusive, the makers of friends,
the bountiful, those rid of stinginess,
those who lead, train, and persuade:
they’re the kind who win glory.
Giving and kindly words,
taking care here,
and treating equally in worldly conditions,
as they deserve in each case;
these ways of being inclusive in the world
are like a moving chariot’s linchpin.
If there were no such ways of being inclusive,
neither mother nor father
would be respected and honored
for what they’ve done for their children.
But since these ways of being inclusive do exist,
the astute do regard them well,
so they achieve greatness
and are praised.”

text:
Reposting the quoted text from Korinji's social media, video linked below.

If you'd like and are able to help fund the final foundational infrastructure establishing the first fully-fledged, traditional Rinzai Zen training monastery in America, please do!

"In this video kicking off a new fundraising campaign, Meido Roshi speaks about the history of Korinji, the many acts of generosity and sacrifice that have made it possible, and the final step now before us: completing the hojo, the abbot's residence.

The hojo will help ensure that Korinji can continue as a living place of Zen training and transmission for future generations.

If you feel moved, please support this effort to bring the monastery to completion. One way you can contribute is by joining the Patreon, or (if you are already a member) increasing your donation tier there. Another is by contributing directly. Donations of any amount, no matter how small, are welcome and deeply meaningful. Every offering helps carry our work forward.

And even if you cannot donate, send us your good wishes and, above all, continue to practice. Practice is the most profound gift that any of us can give, to one another and to all beings.

With gratitude to all of you!"

text:
Last month someone asked for recommendations of female jhana teachers. I've mostly gravitated towards male teachers across the lineages I've participated/participate in. Some archetypal resonance of the positive embodiment I aspire to emulate perhaps accounts for it, as well as what seems a general gender-disparity in the teachers that are available. Regardless of the reason, for longer-term mentorship especially, it's important to find a teacher/teachers who you can connect with.

These are some of the female teachers I know and/or have heard from those I trust are excellent. Please add other recommendations below. My knowledge and network are far from complete!

For jhana/Theravada and Theravada-influenced more broadly:

Leigh Brasington has recommended Mary Aubry, for whom I didn't find contact info but you may be able to (https://t.co/BUVHW6uJzy), and Rachel O'Brien (https://t.co/0AWHgKiApQ).

In Pa-Auk's lineage there's Tina Rasmussen, (https://t.co/Usnf0rSE6E), Beth Upton (https://t.co/nQ6X8ilIW5), Shaila Catherine (https://t.co/YNNfdnE3W5), and Nikki Mirghafori (https://t.co/X8CzScA51G).

For Zen, I worked 1:1 with Hojin Sensei at ZMM during sesshin a fair bit, and she's in Brooklyn most of the time, (https://t.co/hcgFidGFEd), there's Hidden Valley Zen Center with Sozui Roshi (https://t.co/kLOSSIc5iN), and Cristina Moon at Chozen-ji, (https://t.co/00iZjit8hE).

For Vajrayana and Dzogchen, Lama Lena (https://t.co/cvXqbD6aL7), Anne Klein (https://t.co/cvXqbD6aL7), and @_awbery_ of course on the experimental, non-binary/nondual end.

Cheers!

text:
"From the emptiness perspective, there is no arising, no ceasing, no coming, no going, no restraint, no freedom, no support, no abiding, no saṃsāra and no nirvāṇa, no adoption, no rejection, no emptiness, no non-emptiness, no reality, no attributes, no names, no words, no sounds, no conventions, no limits, no centre, no intrinsic nature from the very beginning, and a transcendence of words, thoughts and description.

From the appearance perspective, there is awareness; there is clarity; there are kāyas; there are wisdoms; there are enlightened qualities; there is enlightened activity; there are pure realms; there are divine palaces; there are principal deities and retinues; and there are all the qualities of perfection in their entirety."

- Longchenpa

text:
There's a rank eternalism rampant in much dualistic Dharma derived from his discourses I imagine old Sid would have no truck with.

Wisely turning from senseless, cyclical, afflicted attraction and aversion to sensual objects and thereby abandoning what undergirds the ignorance of a subject is aces, but if on the way a countervailing rejection and aversion to appearance develops and entrenches in opposition and results in reification of a dimension, realm, state, element, whatever mistaken concretization fixates, the cure has curdled into the very same disease.

Nibbana isn't something someplace else.

All old hat, all my complaints, but the same confusions crop up over and again, so out with it.

text:
"As we look at the objects and subjective experiences of the six sensory perceptions and at subtle and coarse conceptuality, we see that they lack true reality, and they are liberated like rainbows vanishing in the sky. They arise unceasingly in separate forms from a single source, but they do not stray from the space-like essence, naturally arisen wisdom, and movement is purified and liberated there and then. The Union of the Sun and Moon says:

For whoever realizes that apparent phenomena
Lack true reality,
Appearance and emptiness are non-dual.
Sixfold perception is free and unconstrained."

- the Longest of Chenpas

text:
Dancing and singing in the shower just now, somatic memories surfacing of how I used to brace against and inhibit certain ways my body would want to flow through space due to early encoding as feminine and therefore unsafe to enact. Internalized homophobia is no fun.

Where before knotty brambles bound up in tissues muffled the swaying and singing of sinews, now undirected expression in any arrangement articulates.

Undoing, undone, what fun, what fun!

text:
"There was a disciple of a certain Buddhist priest who said that he practiced zazen day and night; that there was no distinction between himself and others, and that there was no birth-and-death. I asked him of his satori, and although he was extremely apprehensive about answering, he said, “It is beyond someone like me.”

To be a Buddhist master is by no means a trifling matter. Above all, the prac­tice of the Way of Buddha is difficult without a master. Those who practice zazen suffer acutely from the awareness that zazen and their everyday life are not in harmony. They say that right now there is no distinction between the things before their eyes and themselves. But that is absurd. People hate to eat miso that stinks of miso.

...

To a person who is deeply deluded:

You who are not aware
Of your body’s deluding you
Are the same fellow
Who fears foxes and badgers.

...

To someone sincerely seeking the Way:
Originally it cannot be taught or learned;
When you do not know it
It is unknown;
When you know it
It is still unknown."

- Shido Bunan

- Shido Bunan

text:
"There was a disciple of a certain Buddhist priest who said that he practiced zazen day and night; that there was no distinction between himself and others, and that there was no birth-and-death. I asked him of his satori, and although he was extremely apprehensive about answering, he said, “It is beyond someone like me.”

To be a Buddhist master is by no means a trifling matter. Above all, the prac­tice of the Way of Buddha is difficult without a master. Those who practice zazen suffer acutely from the awareness that zazen and their everyday life are not in harmony. They say that right now there is no distinction between the things before their eyes and themselves. But that is absurd. People hate to eat miso that stinks of miso.

...

To a person who is deeply deluded:

You who are not aware
Of your body’s deluding you
Are the same fellow
Who fears foxes and badgers.

...

To someone sincerely seeking the Way:

Originally it cannot be taught or learned;
When you do not know it
It is unknown;
When you know it
It is still unknown."

- Shido Bunan

text:
"If you open your mind to the idea that the Buddha was actually advocating ego development instead of egolessness, you see that there’s nothing lopsided or lacking in his understanding of healthy ego functioning.  In fact, he mastered some ego skills that Western psychology has yet to explore, such as how to use right concentration to satisfy the desire for immediate pleasure; how to develop an integrated sense of causality that ultimately makes a sense of self superfluous; how to harness the ego’s drive for lasting happiness so that it leads to a happiness transcending space and time."

text:
I'm grateful to have been immersed in and informed by tradition so that when I started seeing lights, having heavenly and hellish visions, wild body-schema distortions, eruptions of somatic energy, entity encounters, shifts in identity, waking and dreaming life bleeding together and at times losing all distinction, and all manner of other transformations, and occasional cessations, of consciousness, I was able in every case to refer to contextualize and integrate all the weird shit with a relative minimum of distress and disorientation.
"Ah, yep, just as it says in the manuals. Nothing exceptional or overly worrying. Check it and discuss with senior friends and teachers when possible and carry on, no bother."
I can only express my gratitude to my teachers and the rich traditions they maintain. I doubt I would have done well in their absence!

text:
Started reading, good overview and collection from diverse sources!

"...some of these texts might provide meditation teachers with practical and actionable techniques that could be taught to students in retreat settings to help prevent or ameliorate the kinds of disastrous side effects discussed in the opening of this chapter. I believe it may also be possible for meditators to self-administer these or similar techniques at the first sign of symptoms, before they are overcome by anxiety, depression, psychosis, and other severe psychological and physical ailments. Likewise, I believe that those working outside Buddhist frameworks—whether medical doctors, psychiatric professionals, or practitioners of contemporary Asian medical systems such as acupuncture or yoga therapy—might also be able to adapt some of these methods to enhance their current repertoires of interventions. I believe that these texts could also contribute to the development of diagnostic models that help modern clinicians and researchers discriminate between normal signs of meditation progress, adverse effects that can be managed by making changes to the meditation regimen, and genuine psychiatric crises."

text: "As meditation becomes ever more popular in the West, it is being learned primarily via apps, websites, workplace workshops, self-retreats, AI guidance, and countless other ways outside traditional Buddhist institutional structures. That means that a growing number of practitioners will not only inevitably experience mental and physical symptoms caused or exacerbated by practices they were led to believe could only be beneficial, but also that they will not necessarily turn to Buddhist teachers or communities for support or remedies to their ailments."

text:
"Let us not hold to the black-and-white position that either meditation is an unassailable good or it should be thrown out entirely just because there may be some undesirable side effects. That being said, given the materials presented in this volume, I do believe it is an ethical imperative that researchers, teachers, and practitioners educate themselves about the dangers and reflect on how Asian perspectives might inform contemporary Western approaches to meditation. I also believe that discovering within these pages new frameworks that make sense of anomalous meditation experiences may benefit sufferers. Just knowing that meditators from Buddhist traditions all over the world have, through no fault of their own, encountered challenging experiences and found solutions may, in itself, be therapeutic. In these many ways, I hope that this book can serve as a catalyst for a wider discussion to take place and thus contribute to the alleviation of suffering."

👏👏👏

text:
Moving beyond jhana-limerence does not equate to the breaking of the fetters of lusting for form and formlessness. Whether apparent to you or not, if any action of body, speech, or mind evinces reification of and attachment or aversion to any form gross or subtle or any more refined perception or state, no.

It's not my favorite, but as the fetters model has seen some of the worst abuse and terminological confusion, I'm impelled to implore you to look to the original sources and allow the possibility that the texts mean what they say and that the Buddha found freedom far in excess of the appropriative, diluted, and misinformed interpretations which paint awakening as a mere absence of gross surface-psychological reactivity.

"The experience of the yogi is then this: The world is seen as the mystical maṇḍala and all living beings as tantric deities; everything that one eats and drinks becomes transformed into blissful ambrosia; all of one’s activities become spiritual, regardless of how they conventionally appear; and every sound that one makes becomes part of a great vajra [adamantine] song.

I, a tantric yogin, have a blissful mind; I, a tantric yogin, spontaneously generate goodness in everything I do. All male divinities dance within me and all female divinities channel their sacred vajra songs through me."

text:
More teisho have been released from Korinji lately. Appreciating Meido Roshi's discussion of Torei's Kaibaban, "A Whip for a Good Horse", which reminds of this from the early Canon on how a Tathagata kills:

"Then Kesin the horse trainer went to the Blessed One and, on arrival, having bowed down, sat to one side. As he was sitting there, the Blessed One said to him: “You, Kesin, are well known as a trainer of tamable horses. How do you train a tamable horse?”

“Lord, I train a tamable horse with mildness, with harshness, with both mildness & harshness.”

“And if a tamable horse doesn’t submit either to a mild training or to a harsh training or to a mild & harsh training, Kesin, what do you do?”

“If a tamable horse doesn’t submit either to a mild training or to a harsh training or to a mild and harsh training, lord, then I kill it. Why is that? ‘Don’t let this be a disgrace to my lineage of teachers.’ But the Blessed One, lord, is the unexcelled trainer of tamable people. How do you train a tamable person?”

“Kesin, I train a tamable person with mildness, with harshness, with both mildness & harshness.

“In using mildness, ‘Such is good bodily conduct. Such is the result of good bodily conduct. Such is good verbal conduct. Such is the result of good verbal conduct. Such is good mental conduct. Such is the result of good mental conduct. Such are the devas. Such are human beings.’

“In using harshness, ‘Such is bodily misconduct. Such is the result of bodily misconduct. Such is verbal misconduct. Such is the result of verbal misconduct. Such is mental misconduct. Such is the result of mental misconduct. Such is hell. Such is the animal womb. Such the realm of the hungry ghosts.’

“In using mildness & harshness, ‘Such is good bodily conduct. Such is the result of good bodily conduct. Such is bodily misconduct. Such is the result of bodily misconduct. Such is good verbal conduct. Such is the result of good verbal conduct. Such is verbal misconduct. Such is the result of verbal misconduct. Such is good mental conduct. Such is the result of good mental conduct. Such is mental misconduct. Such is the result of mental misconduct. Such are the devas. Such are human beings. Such is hell. Such is the animal womb. Such the realm of the hungry ghosts.’”

“And if a tamable person doesn’t submit either to a mild training or to a harsh training or to a mild & harsh training, what do you do?”

“If a tamable person doesn’t submit either to a mild training or to a harsh training or to a mild & harsh training, then I kill him, Kesin.”

“But it’s not proper for our Blessed One to take life! And yet the Blessed One just said, ‘I kill him, Kesin.’”

“It is true, Kesin, that it’s not proper for a Tathāgata to take life. But if a tamable person doesn’t submit either to a mild training or to a harsh training or to a mild & harsh training, then the Tathāgata doesn’t regard him as being worth speaking to or admonishing. His observant companions in the holy life don’t regard him as being worth speaking to or admonishing. This is what it means to be totally destroyed in the Dhamma & Vinaya, when the Tathāgata doesn’t regard one as being worth speaking to or admonishing, and one’s observant companions in the holy life don’t regard one as being worth speaking to or admonishing.”

“But it’s not proper for our Blessed One to take life! And yet the Blessed One just said, ‘I kill him, Kesin.’”

“It is true, Kesin, that it’s not proper for a Tathāgata to take life. But if a tamable person doesn’t submit either to a mild training or to a harsh training or to a mild & harsh training, then the Tathāgata doesn’t regard him as being worth speaking to or admonishing. His observant companions in the holy life don’t regard him as being worth speaking to or admonishing. This is what it means to be totally destroyed in the Dhamma & Vinaya, when the Tathāgata doesn’t regard one as being worth speaking to or admonishing, and one’s observant companions in the holy life don’t regard one as being worth speaking to or admonishing.”

text:
Father's day yesterday, caught up with dear old Dad briefly, causing reflection on how clearly rooted in early childhood and adolescent developmental dynamics my passionate obsession with Dharma has been.

Dependent origination does its dance!

For most of my early childhood my father was still actively drinking, a habit he picked up in his teenage years to blunt emotional pain stemming in large part from his own family's foibles. I remember love and tenderness, but also a pronounced absence, and a confusing incongruity between his performance of his role as beloved parish priest and how he manifested at home. I never made the connection, and only realized he had had a drinking problem years later when my parents separated and he had been sober for some years already. Around then, at the onset of puberty and a dawning awareness of the complexity and pain shot through the web of life, I fell into a suicidal depression lasting some seven years.

I trusted no one and nothing, became bitterly cynical, self-hating, misanthropic, addicted myself to screens, philosophy, pornography, video-games, and gorged learning every ill of history, smugly self-satisfied even as I was disgusted within and without.

Then, when encountering the Buddha's Dharma, unsurprisingly, I threw myself in heedless and headlong like a lost child returning home, anxious and clingy, aflame, burning in devotion and desirous of awakening or else death in the attempt.

Drama-queen, me. As I've made more of my own mistakes, I sympathize with him more and it's easier to forgive it all, him, myself, and everyone.

And so, yesterday, my old man joked about a funeral he'd been to where the child opened with,

"Well, we all knew Dad was a bastard."

I assured him I'd give a good eulogy, and thanked him for the gift of this life and all his love.

Thanks to you, Dad. We're all doing our best.❤️

text:
Break open,
Open as the sky.

Even as pain paints the stars,
There is Nothing,
Nothing to fear.

Space a scar healed in time,
Nature nurtured from birth,
One breath,
Death, less, and even so,
No one does know the how,
And the coming goes, goes,
Gone going it flows.

text:
A little over a year since we separated, now.
Six years, together, before.
We talked for the first time in a while the other day about the car, the cat, and taxes, about shared sangha and teachers, and meeting up in the Fall to catch up properly.
Guests in each other's lives.
Wounding and healing, understanding and confusion all intermixed.
Regrets, aspirations, smoke in the breeze.

A turbulent year: four months and more on retreat, 5 countries, never in the same place for long, a torrid rebound followed by further error enjoining committing to a prolonged return to celibacy.

Without roadmap or fixed direction, yet more resolution than at any time prior there's clarity and acceptance for what's here, and what there is to do.

Love and see, within and without, no doubt, no doubt.

text: "These life-stories teach the Dharma. So if you hear them many times and keep them in mind, they will help you to understand what to do and what not to do. You will know which path to follow, without any mistake. Faith will be born in those with no faith and be enhanced in those who have faith; diligence, intelligence, revulsion toward samsara, and other excellent qualities will grow effortlessly. Your fervent devotion will enable you to receive the blessings of the true lineage; meditation experiences and realization not yet born will arise, and those already born will increase [11b]. When you think, ‘May I perform the same wondrous deeds as these Bodhisattvas!’ Bodhicitta will arise. When you rejoice in their great virtuous acts, you will gain the same merit as if you had performed them yourself. When you hear the profound accounts of the Bodhisattvas’ wondrous deeds, your attachment and aggression and other negative thoughts, together with suffering of all kinds, will stop, while your joy and pure deeds will blossom. Even if you are criticized and slandered by people with wrong views, you will be welcomed by great beings, and, having been accepted as their disciple, you will put an end to samsara. In short, these life-stories are a source of benefit for both yourself and others. To read, hear, or reflect on them is very meaningful; you should read, copy, listen to, memorize and reflect upon them with diligence. Keep them in mind and put them into practice with ardent devotion and respect."
